summary: Reviewed 27 files across src/app/, src/components/shell/, src/styles/, and src/main.tsx (approximately 5,600 lines of production code plus styles). The shell architecture is well-decomposed, with clear ownership boundaries between shell-data (bootstrap + state), shell (composition), shell-tasks (archive task lifecycle), and shell-runtime-status (polling). The paper aesthetic is faithfully implemented with correct three-font system, CJK locale overrides, and theme switching. Key issues found: design-tokens.md vs tokens.css color discrepancies, missing notification queue button in topbar (per screens-and-nav.md), duplicated helper functions between shell-data.tsx and shell-data-actions.ts, inconsistent error boundary coverage across routes, a transition token value mismatch with the spec, and the profile scope switcher being in the status bar instead of the topbar as specified. Several routes lack ErrorBoundary protection.

findings.title: Topbar missing notification queue button specified in screens-and-nav.md
findings.description: screens-and-nav.md section 'Navigation' line 99-100 specifies: 'Topbar right side provides notification queue button; unread notifications use low-interference badge/highlight, opening the panel marks read, each notification has timestamp and can be individually dismissed.' The topbar implementation has no notification button, badge, or panel. The shell-data.tsx tracks notifications (with ShellNotification[], unreadNotificationCount, markNotificationsRead, dismissNotification) but there is no UI surface rendering them. Notifications are persisted to localStorage but never shown to the user.
findings.impact: Users have no way to see backup completions, schedule health warnings, or import results that arrive as notifications. The notification infrastructure exists in shell-data but is invisible.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a notification bell button with unread badge to PKTopbar between the search palette button and the Backup Now CTA. Wire it to a popover that renders the notification queue from useShellData(). The spec says it should be to the left of the profile scope switcher and Backup Now.

findings.title: Profile scope switcher is in status bar, not in topbar as specified
findings.description: screens-and-nav.md line 100 states: 'The last two controls on the top-right must be the shared profile scope switcher and the Backup now main CTA.' The current implementation places the profile scope switcher in the PKStatusBar (bottom bar), not in the PKTopbar. The topbar right side only contains the search palette trigger and Backup Now button.
findings.impact: The profile scope control is less discoverable in the status bar than it would be in the topbar. Users may not realize they can filter their view by browser profile. However, the status bar placement does provide functional parity - users can switch profiles from the bottom bar.
findings.suggestedFix: Either move the profile scope switcher to the topbar right side (between notification queue and Backup Now), or update screens-and-nav.md to reflect the current status-bar placement as a deliberate design decision.

findings.title: Token color values diverge from design-tokens.md contract
findings.description:
design-tokens.md is declared as the contract that tokens.css implements, and explicitly states 'If tokens.css and this file disagree, fix tokens.css to match this contract -- not the other way around.' Several values diverge:

- Light --bg-paper: doc says #ece7de, tokens.css has #f6f3ed (doc's --bg-paper value is actually at --bg-page in tokens.css)
- Light --bg-card-paper: doc says #f6f3ed, tokens.css uses --bg-card: #fdfcf9 (mismatch)
- Light --bg-page: doc says #fdfcf9, tokens.css has #ece7de (these are swapped vs doc)
- Light --ink: doc says #171513, tokens.css has #1c1814
- Light --ink-secondary: doc says #3c3a36, tokens.css has #4a4139
- Light --ink-muted: doc says #6a655c, tokens.css has #887d6e
- Light --ink-faint: doc says #9a948a, tokens.css has #beb3a2
- Light --ink-ghost: doc says #bdb6a9, tokens.css has #d5cdc0
- Light --border-light: doc says #e6e2d8, tokens.css has #e4ddd2
- Light --border-default: doc says #d6d1c4, tokens.css has #d5cdc0
- Light --accent-text: doc says #2c4360, tokens.css has var(--accent)
- Light --danger: doc says #a03821, tokens.css uses --error: #a84040
- Dark mode values also have multiple mismatches.

The naming scheme also diverges: doc uses --bg-paper/--bg-card-paper/--bg-page, while tokens.css uses --bg-page/--bg-paper/--bg-card.
findings.impact: The design system contract document and the actual runtime tokens tell different stories. This makes it impossible for other contributors to know which values are authoritative. The doc explicitly says it is the contract. If the code evolved intentionally, the doc needs updating.
findings.suggestedFix: Audit each divergence: if tokens.css was tuned deliberately for visual quality, update design-tokens.md to match. If tokens.css drifted accidentally, fix tokens.css to match the doc. Also reconcile the naming scheme (--bg-paper vs --bg-card-paper vs --bg-page).

findings.title: Transition token value does not match design-tokens.md spec
findings.description: design-tokens.md specifies --transition as '120ms ease', but tokens.css sets it to '150ms ease'. The doc is Accepted and explicitly says it is the contract.
findings.impact: Low visual impact - 30ms difference is barely perceptible. But it is a contract violation per the doc's own rules.
findings.suggestedFix: Change tokens.css to 120ms or update design-tokens.md to 150ms if the higher value was a deliberate tuning.

findings.title: Status bar height token (32px) does not match design-tokens.md spec (28px)
findings.description: design-tokens.md specifies --layout-status-bar-height as 28px. tokens.css defines --statusbar-h: 32px. The status bar component renders at h-[32px]. The spec says 28px.
findings.impact: 4px difference in the status bar height. Minor visual discrepancy, but it is a contract mismatch.
findings.suggestedFix: Reconcile: either update tokens.css to 28px or update the spec to 28px + 4px density padding.

findings.title: Duplicate isSafariAccessIssueMessage and backupCompletionNotice functions
findings.description: isSafariAccessIssueMessage is defined identically in both shell-data.tsx (line 769) and shell-data-actions.ts (line 91). Similarly, backup completion notice logic is duplicated: backupCompletionNoticeForTask in shell-data.tsx (line 298) and backupCompletionNotice in shell-data-actions.ts (line 98). Both do essentially the same thing with the same SafariAccessIssue/backup-notice pattern.
findings.impact: If a new Safari access pattern needs to be recognized, it must be updated in two places. Risk of silent divergence.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract isSafariAccessIssueMessage and backupCompletionNotice into shell-data-helpers.ts (which already exists for this purpose) and import from both consumers.

findings.title: Most routes lack ErrorBoundary protection
findings.description: Only explorer, search, intelligence (and its sub-routes), and jobs have ErrorBoundary: ShellRouteErrorBoundary. Six routes lack it: import (line 392), audit (line 400), assistant (line 384), schedule (line 418), security (line 434), settings (line 450), integrations (line 426), and maintenance (line 442). The dashboard index route (line 277) also lacks it. If any of these routes throw during render, the entire shell crashes rather than showing the graceful ShellRouteErrorBoundary recovery UI.
findings.impact: A runtime error in any unprotected route tears down the entire shell, losing all shell chrome (sidebar, status bar, notifications). Users see React's raw error screen instead of a recovery UI with retry/navigate-home options. Import and settings are high-traffic routes where errors are most likely during backend communication.
findings.suggestedFix: Add ErrorBoundary: ShellRouteErrorBoundary to every route object in appRouteChildren. The boundary already handles route errors gracefully with retry, Jobs link, and Dashboard link.

findings.title: Search palette Cmd+Enter sends to /explorer instead of /search
findings.description: The palette's 'full search' action (Cmd+Enter) navigates to /explorer?q=... but the router has a dedicated /search route that shows the search surface of ExplorerPage. The hint text says 'full search' which maps conceptually to the Search page, not the Browse view of Explorer.
findings.impact: Minor UX inconsistency. The user goes to Explorer Browse view with a query filter rather than the Search-specific surface. Functionally both work, but the sidebar will highlight Explorer rather than Search, which might confuse users who expect the full search experience.
findings.suggestedFix: Change the navigation target to /search?q=... so the sidebar highlights Search and the ExplorerPage mounts in search mode.

findings.title: Sidebar nav uses both NavLink isActive and prop-based activeId, risking double-highlight
findings.description: The NavLink className callback checks both `isActive` (from React Router's URL matching) and `activeId === item.id` (from the prop passed by the shell). These are not always consistent - e.g., if route matching logic produces a different answer than the handle-based active screen resolution in shell.tsx. The OR condition `isActive || activeId === item.id` means either can trigger the highlight. For intelligence sub-routes, NavLink isActive would be false for the parent /intelligence item, but activeId would still be 'intelligence' from the handle - so it works. But in edge cases (e.g., a redirect), the two sources could disagree and produce confusing visual state.
findings.impact: Low-probability visual inconsistency. The current behavior mostly works because both signals agree. But it is a fragile coupling.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider using only one source of truth for active state. Either rely entirely on activeId from the shell (and drop the NavLink isActive check), or rely on NavLink routing and drop the activeId prop.

findings.title: Missing density-card-padding and density-card-gap tokens
findings.description: design-tokens.md specifies --density-card-padding: 18px and --density-card-gap: 16px as shell density tokens, and mentions a 'Density' toggle in Settings that picks between comfortable (default) and compact (-2px on each --density-* token). tokens.css does not define these tokens, nor the density toggle mechanism.
findings.impact: Card density cannot be toggled between comfortable and compact as the spec promises. Components that should consume these tokens instead use hard-coded spacing.
findings.suggestedFix: Add --density-card-padding and --density-card-gap to the :root block in tokens.css. Add a data-density='compact' selector that reduces each by 2px. Wire through the Settings Appearance toggle.

findings.title: shell-data.tsx at 797 lines is approaching the 800-line new-file limit
findings.description: shell-data.tsx is 797 lines, just under the 800-line cap for initial files. It contains the entire ShellDataProvider including notification persistence, schedule health probes, import orchestration, archive task lifecycle, and idle timer management. The actions were already extracted to shell-data-actions.ts, but import flow orchestration (runImport, lines 592-638) and the notification/task helpers (beginArchiveTask, updateBackupTask, etc.) still live inline.
findings.impact: If any new shell-level responsibility is added, it will cross the 800-line threshold. The file is already complex with many interleaved concerns.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider extracting the import orchestration flow (runImport and its task lifecycle) into a dedicated shell-import-actions.ts, similar to how backup actions were extracted into shell-data-actions.ts.

findings.title: Onboarding shell still uses v0.2 brutalist CSS classes
findings.description: OnboardingShell uses legacy CSS classes: app-frame, onboarding-frame, shell-dot-grid, corner-mark, onboarding-header, logo-lockup, logo-mark, logo-name, logo-version, onboarding-content, btn-secondary. These come from the v0.2 brutalist era. The onboarding shell has not been migrated to the paper aesthetic. It also imports BrandMark from '../components/brand-mark' (the old logo) instead of PKBrandMark from the shell components.
findings.impact: The onboarding experience has a visually different aesthetic from the rest of the app. Users going through first-time setup see a different visual language than the paper shell they will use daily. This is already tracked in BACKLOG as part of WORK-V03-PAPER-REMAINING-ROUTES-INNER noting that onboarding inner steps were reskinned but the shell frame was not.
findings.suggestedFix: Restyle OnboardingShell to use the paper aesthetic (bg-paper, font-serif for branding, PKBrandMark, paper token classes). Already partially tracked in BACKLOG.

findings.title: ShellRouteErrorBoundary uses legacy v0.2 CSS class names
findings.description: ShellRouteErrorBoundary uses page-shell, btn-secondary, panel, panel-header, panel-title, panel-body, code-block -- all legacy v0.2 CSS class names. When the error boundary renders inside the paper shell, its visual appearance may not match the paper aesthetic.
findings.impact: Minor visual inconsistency only visible during error states. But error states are trust-critical moments per UX principles, so looking out-of-place erodes confidence.
findings.suggestedFix: Migrate to paper tokens and Tailwind utilities: bg-paper, font-serif, PaperCard, etc.

commendations.title: Excellent shell decomposition with clear ownership boundaries
commendations.description: The shell architecture splits concerns into well-defined files: shell.tsx (pure composition), shell-data.tsx (state provider), shell-data-actions.ts (mutation callbacks), shell-data-helpers.ts (pure logic), shell-tasks.ts (task lifecycle), shell-runtime-status.ts (polling). Each file has a doc header stating Responsibilities and Not Responsible For. This makes the shell maintainable despite its complexity.

commendations.title: Robust three-font system with proper CJK locale fallbacks
commendations.description: tokens.css implements the three-font contract (serif/sans/mono) with carefully curated CJK stacks for zh-CN and zh-TW via :root:lang() selectors. The data-fonts='system' override correctly drops bundled families while preserving CJK stacks. fonts.css bundles only Latin subsets via @fontsource, avoiding CDN dependency. The html[lang] is set before first paint in main.tsx.

commendations.title: Route history navigation hook is thoughtfully engineered
commendations.description: use-route-history-nav.ts handles the notoriously tricky problem of tracking back/forward state in React Router. It correctly distinguishes goForward Pops from goBack Pops via a navIntentRef intent ring, prevents first-render underflow, handles editable-target filtering, and binds Cmd+[/] shortcuts. The doc comments explain each design decision including why window.history.state.idx cannot be trusted.

commendations.title: Shell task store correctly implements single-writer archive constraint
commendations.description: shell-tasks.ts and the beginArchiveTask pattern in shell-data.tsx enforce the AGENTS.md rule that only one archive-write task (import or backup) can run at a time. The second attempt gets a notification with a link to the running task. Task logs are bounded (shellTaskLogLimit = 160), notifications are capped (50), and both use immutable state updates suitable for React.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Notification queue has no rendering surface anywhere in the app
crossModuleConcerns.description: ShellDataProvider maintains a full notification lifecycle (add, mark-read, dismiss, persist to localStorage) but no component in the entire app renders these notifications. The PKTopbar and PKSidebar never display unreadNotificationCount. FA-DASHBOARD and any focus area covering the topbar should check whether a notification popover component exists elsewhere.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-DASHBOARD, FA-EXPLORER, all pages that consume useShellData

crossModuleConcerns.title: Shell busy overlay vs BackgroundProgress rendering logic
crossModuleConcerns.description: shell.tsx (lines 328-345) decides between BusyOverlay (blocking) and BackgroundProgress (non-blocking) based on busyOverlay.background. The BackgroundProgress component is imported from @/components/primitives/. Any focus area reviewing those primitives should verify the bottom-bar strip renders correctly alongside the status bar and that z-index layering does not conflict.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Profile scope wiring between shell and route consumers
crossModuleConcerns.description: The shell reads useProfileScope().activeProfileId from the status bar source picker. Routes that consume this scope (Explorer, Intelligence, Dashboard) need to honor the selectedSourceId consistently. Any focus area reviewing these pages should verify they actually call useProfileScope() and filter their data accordingly.

summary: Reviewed 68 non-test files totaling ~15,883 lines across src/pages/explorer/ and src/components/explorer-paper/. The Explorer surface is a well-architected, deeply-considered recall workbench. The paper-redesign code quality is high: clean module boundaries, thorough doc comments, good separation of concerns between route orchestration (index.tsx), view composition (paper-view.tsx), and presentation (paper-contact-sheet.tsx). The viewport-driven day-recycling virtualization, directional prefetch, and the cascading day-insights cache are exemplary. Key concerns include a regex validation false-positive for Rust named groups, a performance scaling concern in the O(N) combinedTimeResults memo at scale, the paper-contact-sheet.tsx file approaching the 1000-line two-phase review threshold, and a detail panel flush-on-close race with React StrictMode double-invoke.

findings.title: Regex validation falsely rejects Rust named capture groups
findings.description: The `unsupportedRustRegexFragments` array includes `'(?<'` to reject JS look-behind `(?<=` and `(?<!`. However, Rust's regex crate supports named capture groups via `(?<name>...)`, and this check rejects those too. A user typing `(?<year>\d{4})` would see 'Invalid regex' even though the Rust backend would accept it. The check correctly blocks look-around (`(?<=`, `(?<!`) since those appear earlier in the array, but the fallthrough `'(?<'` is overly broad.
findings.impact: Users who know Rust regex named capture syntax will get a false 'Invalid regex' block on the client side, preventing legitimate queries from reaching the backend.
findings.suggestedFix: Remove `'(?<'` from the fragment list. The look-behind patterns `'(?<='` and `'(?<!'` already appear as separate entries and will catch those cases. Alternatively, replace the `'(?<'` entry with a negative check that only blocks `(?<` when NOT followed by an identifier char and `>` (i.e., only block `(?<=` and `(?<!`).
findings.codeSnippet:
const unsupportedRustRegexFragments = [
  '(?=',
  '(?!',
  '(?<=',
  '(?<!',
  '(?<',   // <-- also blocks Rust named groups (?<name>...)
  '\\k<',
]

findings.title: O(N) full-array map on every favicon/og-image hydration pass at scale
findings.description: The `renderedTimeResults` memo spreads and maps the entire `combinedTimeResults.items` array on every change to `faviconCache` or `ogImageCache`. With the MAX_ACCUMULATED_PAGES at 500 and ~50 rows/page, this is ~25,000 items. Each icon lookup also calls `historyFaviconLookupKey()` (string concatenation) and `historyOgImageLookupKey()` per row. At the 500-page cap, each hydration pass does ~75,000 string ops plus ~25,000 object spreads. The BACKLOG already notes this as the reason the cap was lowered from 1,000 to 500, but even at 500 pages this will cause visible jank on the target 4-core/8GB machine during frequent hydration bursts (e.g., rapid scrolling that triggers multiple icon loads per second).
findings.impact: On the target 4-core / 8 GB machine with a large archive, favicon/og-image hydration at deep scroll depths (200+ pages) could cause 50-200ms main-thread blocking per hydration pass, degrading scroll smoothness.
findings.suggestedFix: Implement an incremental hydration overlay: instead of re-mapping the entire items array, maintain a separate sparse overlay Map of hydrated entries and merge at render time in the contact sheet. This is the 'incremental aggregator' the BACKLOG already identifies as the precondition for raising the cap.

findings.title: Detail panel unmount-flush may double-fire in React StrictMode
findings.description: The unmount cleanup effect (lines 245-258) flushes pending notes via `pendingFlushRef.current` and clears the timer. In React 18+ StrictMode (which PathKeep uses via Vite defaults), effects mount-unmount-remount. On the first unmount, the effect flushes the pending value. On the remount, `pendingFlushRef.current` is already null, so the second mount starts clean. However, if a user is actively typing when StrictMode double-invokes, the first unmount flush writes the intermediate value, and the remount resets `saveTimer` to null (which is correct), but the `notesValue` state survives across StrictMode remounts. This means the intermediate write goes through but the debounce timer that would have fired for the final value is lost. In development mode only, this could cause the last few characters of a note to be lost if typing at the exact moment of a StrictMode re-render.
findings.impact: Development-only data loss scenario: notes may lose the last typed characters during a StrictMode remount cycle. Production builds are unaffected.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a guard to the unmount cleanup that checks `saveTimer.current !== null` before flushing, ensuring we only flush when a debounce was genuinely in flight rather than on a StrictMode teardown where the timer was already cleared.

findings.title: paper-contact-sheet.tsx at 963 lines, approaching the 1000-line two-phase review threshold
findings.description: Per AGENTS.md, any file over 1000 lines requires a two-phase review before refactoring, and files over 1200 lines trigger a BACKLOG maintainability task. At 963 lines, this file is 37 lines from the threshold. It contains the main PaperContactSheet component, PaperDayBlock, PaperDayBlockContent, DayListSkeleton, InfiniteScrollFooter, PaginationFooter, renderCardBlock, flattenBlocks, toListEntry, formatTimeFromVisitTime, formatRange, and describeDayInLanguage -- 12 distinct functions/components with mixed responsibilities (composition, formatting, rendering).
findings.impact: Any new feature addition (e.g., keyboard navigation, a11y improvements, new block types) will push this file past 1000 lines and trigger the mandatory two-phase review requirement.
findings.suggestedFix: Proactively extract the pagination/infinite-scroll footer components and the formatting helpers (formatTimeFromVisitTime, formatRange, describeDayInLanguage) into separate files. The footers are self-contained and already have clear interfaces; extracting them would drop the file to ~750 lines with room to grow.

findings.title: groupEntriesByDay creates a full sorted copy of all entries on every memo invalidation
findings.description: `groupEntriesByDay` begins with `const sorted = [...entries].sort(...)`, creating a full copy of the entries array and sorting it. This runs inside a `useMemo` in paper-view.tsx (line 318) that invalidates whenever `entries` changes. With infinite scroll accumulating up to 25,000 entries, each new page append triggers a full re-sort of the growing array. The sort is O(N log N) but the copy is O(N) allocation, and at 25k entries with ~600 bytes per entry, each call allocates ~15 MB of transient objects.
findings.impact: On the target machine, each page-load during infinite scroll triggers a 15 MB transient allocation + O(N log N) sort of all accumulated entries. This contributes to GC pressure and may cause brief frame drops.
findings.suggestedFix: Since entries arrive pre-sorted from the backend (newest first per page), an incremental merge-insert or a pre-sorted assumption check would avoid the full re-sort. Alternatively, maintain the grouped structure incrementally: append new pages to the existing day/session structure rather than regrouping from scratch.

findings.title: Detail panel shows identical firstVisitAt and lastVisitAt for every entry
findings.description: The mount component maps `selectedEntry.visitedAt` to both `firstVisitAt` and `lastVisitAt` (lines 75-76). The HistoryEntry from the browse query contains only the current visit's timestamp, not the URL's overall first/last visit timestamps. The detail panel's design spec (recall.md section 'Record Detail Panel') calls for showing the actual first and last visit times across all visits to that URL. Currently, both fields always show the same date, which is misleading when a URL has been visited multiple times over months or years.
findings.impact: Users see identical first/last visit dates for every record, which contradicts the recall.md spec and makes the provenance section look broken or redundant for frequently-visited URLs.
findings.suggestedFix: Fetch the full URL visit summary (first visit, last visit, total visits, typed count) from the backend when the detail panel opens. The `query_history` response already carries `visitCount` but not first/last timestamps. Either enrich the browse query response or make a separate `getUrlVisitSummary` call when the panel mounts.

findings.title: Detail panel visit history sparkline is always empty
findings.description: The PaperDetailPanel supports a `visitHistory` prop (an array of date/count rows) and renders a per-visit sparkline (paper-detail-panel.tsx lines 460-490). However, PaperDetailPanelMount never supplies this prop -- the entry shape it constructs at lines 69-80 omits `visitHistory` entirely. The sparkline section is therefore always hidden, despite being fully implemented in the panel component.
findings.impact: A designed and implemented feature (the visit history sparkline) is invisible to users because the mount layer never supplies the data. Users cannot see the per-day visit frequency pattern for any record.
findings.suggestedFix: When the detail panel opens, fetch the URL's visit history from the backend (e.g., a dedicated `getUrlVisitHistory(url)` command or by enriching the browse query response) and pass it as `visitHistory` to the panel entry.

findings.title: Detail panel title version history is never populated
findings.description: PaperDetailPanel supports `titleVersions` (array of date/title pairs) and renders them in a diff-style section (paper-detail-panel.tsx lines 515-531). PaperDetailPanelMount omits this prop, so the title version history section is always hidden. The recall.md spec calls for 'All title versions (if metadata has changed)' in the detail panel.
findings.impact: Users cannot see how a page's title has changed over time, which is one of the specified features of the detail panel per recall.md.
findings.suggestedFix: Supply `titleVersions` from the backend. The archive schema tracks metadata versions; expose them via a dedicated command or enrich the entry shape.

findings.title: IntersectionObserver rootMargin of 1600px may cause excessive prefetching on tall monitors
findings.description: The infinite scroll sentinel uses `rootMargin: '1600px'` (roughly two viewport heights on a 1080p display). On a 4K monitor this is less than one viewport, but on a standard laptop it means the sentinel fires when the user is still two screens away from the bottom. Combined with the directional +2 prefetch in `use-explorer-infinite-pages.ts`, this could result in 3-4 concurrent page fetches queued simultaneously during a moderate scroll, which on an encrypted archive with 0.5-2s per page query could temporarily saturate the worker pool.
findings.impact: On smaller-screen devices, the eager rootMargin may fire prefetches too early, consuming worker pool bandwidth that could serve user-initiated queries (search, filter changes).
findings.suggestedFix: Consider making the rootMargin proportional to the viewport height (e.g., `${window.innerHeight * 1.5}px`) or use a slightly smaller fixed value like 1200px. The directional prefetch already provides the secondary warmup, so the sentinel itself can afford to be less aggressive.

findings.title: Duplicated visitTime ms/s detection logic in two files
findings.description: The `formatTimeFromVisitTime` function in paper-contact-sheet.tsx (line 910) duplicates the `visitTime > 1e12 ? visitTime : visitTime * 1000` heuristic that also exists in `group-entries.ts:visitTimeMs` (line 71). Both independently decide whether a visitTime value is in milliseconds or seconds. If the heuristic needs updating (e.g., different epoch handling), both must be changed in sync.
findings.impact: Risk of divergent behavior if one copy is updated and the other is missed. No current bug, but a maintenance hazard.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract the ms/s normalization into a single shared utility (e.g., in `helpers.ts` or a shared `visit-time.ts` module) and import it from both locations.

findings.title: Search surface formatLocalTime ignores user clock format preference
findings.description: The `formatLocalTime` function used by the Search results surface always formats time as 24-hour (`HH:MM`). Meanwhile, the Browse surface respects the user's 12h/24h clock preference via the `clockFormat` state in paper-view.tsx. A user who has selected 12h clock format will see '2:30 PM' in Browse but '14:30' in Search results for the same visit.
findings.impact: Minor visual inconsistency between Browse and Search surfaces. Users who prefer 12h clock will see mixed formats when switching between the two surfaces.
findings.suggestedFix: Thread the `clockFormat` / `hour12` preference through to PaperSearchPanel and use it in `paperSearchEntryFromHistoryEntry` or in the PaperSearchResult render.

findings.title: Browse day-insights resolve triggers side-effect fetch during render
findings.description: The `resolve` callback triggers a backend fetch as a side effect when it encounters an uncached date (line 168: `request(date)`). This mutates the cache Map during the render phase (line 122: `cacheState.cache.set(date, { state: 'pending' })`). While React currently tolerates side effects in callbacks that happen to run during render, this pattern relies on the in-place Map mutation being invisible to React's reconciler. A future React version with stricter purity enforcement could break this.
findings.impact: Works correctly today but relies on a subtle assumption about React's tolerance of render-phase side effects. The in-place Map mutation is intentionally non-triggering (no state update), which is documented in the code, but it creates fragility.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the fetch trigger to a useEffect that observes the set of visible dates, firing requests for any uncached date. This separates the pure resolve lookup from the impure fetch side effect.

commendations.title: Exemplary viewport-driven day recycling architecture
commendations.description: The viewport mount system (useViewportMount + PaperDayBlock's IntersectionObserver integration) is an elegant solution to the 71k-node DOM problem identified in the BROWSE-VIRT spike. Each day block maintains measured height when recycled, preserving scroll position. The CSS variable approach for sticky day-header offset (--pk-toolbar-h via ResizeObserver) is particularly well-done -- it solves the chip-wrapping regression in a single layout frame with zero React render cycles, which is exactly the right tool for the job.

commendations.title: Thorough debounce + flush lifecycle in the detail panel notes editor
commendations.description: The notes debounce system in PaperDetailPanel (pendingFlushRef + saveTimer + entry-switch flush via useLayoutEffect on trackedEntryId) correctly handles every edge case: mid-edit entry switches flush to the previous URL's handler, unmount flushes any pending edit, and the React 19 render-time setState pattern for tracking entry identity avoids the extra commit cycle of an effect-based approach. The explicit closure capture in handleNotesChange ensures writes always land on the correct URL. This is production-grade auto-save.

commendations.title: Clean separation of URL state, data fetching, and presentation layers
commendations.description: The Explorer route demonstrates excellent architectural discipline. URL state lives in useExplorerUrlState, data fetching in useExplorerData, infinite scroll in useExplorerInfinitePages, favicon/og-image hydration in their own hooks, and presentation in paper-view/paper-contact-sheet. Each hook has clear doc headers with Responsibilities / Not responsible for / Dependencies sections. The pure derivation functions in url-state-derivations.ts are independently testable. This layering makes a 946-line route file readable and reviewable despite its complexity.

crossModuleConcerns.title: SERP og:image suppression list must stay in sync with Rust visit_taxonomy
crossModuleConcerns.description: The `SERP_REGISTRABLE_DOMAINS` and `COMMON_MULTI_LABEL_PUBLIC_SUFFIXES` sets in helpers.ts (lines 253-282) are documented as needing to stay in sync with `vault-core::visit_taxonomy::url::is_search_engine_host` and `COMMON_MULTI_LABEL_PUBLIC_SUFFIXES`. If the Rust lists are updated without updating the JS lists (or vice versa), SERP rows could show misleading og:image icons in Browse while being correctly classified in Rust, or vice versa. FA-BACKEND should verify these lists match.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Desktop annotations hook relies on backend commands that need AppLock session key
crossModuleConcerns.description: useDesktopAnnotations.ts calls `backend.getUrlAnnotation`, `backend.setUrlNotes`, and `backend.replaceUrlTags`, all of which require a valid session database key per the annotations.md spec. If the archive is locked mid-session (e.g., auto-lock timeout), in-flight annotation writes will fail silently (the error is set to lastError but the optimistic cache stays). The detail panel does surface `annotationError` via a 'not saved' alert, but the annotation cache (locallyMutatedRef) will permanently block future hydration for those URLs until the hook is remounted. FA-SHELL should verify the lock/unlock transition properly resets Explorer state.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-SHELL (app-lock session management), FA-BACKEND (annotation command session key handling)

crossModuleConcerns.title: og:image fetch mode config cascading needs Settings-side verification
crossModuleConcerns.description: The explorer route (index.tsx lines 337-348) folds the legacy `fetchEnabled` boolean into the new `fetchMode` tri-state. The og-image hook (use-explorer-og-images.ts line 157) gates `triggerOgImageRefetch` on `fetchMode !== 'off'`. This cascading logic means the Settings page's fetch toggle and the newer fetchMode selector must produce consistent values. FA-SETTINGS should verify that toggling the Settings fetch control correctly propagates to the explorer's effective fetch mode without stale config reads.

summary: Reviewed 65+ source files across src/pages/intelligence/, src/pages/assistant/, src/lib/core-intelligence/, and src/pages/intelligence-surfaces/. The intelligence surface is architecturally well-organized: deterministic Core Intelligence is cleanly separated from optional AI, the overview cache system is sound, and the staged loading pattern avoids the foreground IPC fan-out problem. The assistant page correctly gates AI features behind optionalAiFeaturesAvailable=false with honest disabled messaging. The main concerns are unbounded in-memory cache growth (no eviction/LRU on the three module-level Maps), a potential stale-cache correctness issue where scope changes may serve wrong-scope data before revalidation completes, and some copy.ts fallback-table duplication that will drift from the canonical i18n catalog. File sizes are all within limits (largest is sections.tsx at 744 lines, well under the 800-line cap for newer files).

findings.title: Module-level caches grow without bound, never evicted
findings.description: Three module-scoped Maps (readRequestCache, inflightReadRequests, overviewCache) accumulate entries across every unique (command, args) key and every (dateRange, profileId) scope key. Each time the user changes the time range or profile scope, new entries are created but old ones are never removed. The clearOverviewCache() function exists but is only called on explicit rebuild/clear actions (from Settings/Import), not on routine scope transitions. Over an extended session where a user explores many date ranges or profiles, this will consume increasing heap memory. For the 14.4M records target with multiple profiles, this could materially degrade performance during long sessions.
findings.impact: Unbounded memory growth during extended user sessions exploring different scopes/ranges. On a 4-core/8GB target machine, a power-user session cycling through multiple profiles across day/week/month/quarter/year/all presets could accumulate significant unreclaimable JS heap.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a bounded eviction policy -- either a simple LRU with a maximum entry count (e.g., 20 scope keys for overview, 200 entries for section reads), or a time-based TTL that discards entries older than N minutes. The clearOverviewCache already exists; the fix is to also call it proactively or to cap the Map size at write time.

findings.title: Stale cache served after scope change when warm cache exists from prior scope
findings.description: When a user returns to /intelligence from a sub-route (e.g., domain deep dive) and the previous scope's primary cache is warm, the hook immediately marks primaryReady=true and renders the cached data, then fires a background revalidation with force=true. However, if the user changed the shared profile scope while on the sub-route, the overview state will briefly render the old scope's data until revalidation completes, because the cache is keyed by (dateRange, profileId) but the hasPrimaryCache check on line 92-93 uses the NEW scope parameters to look up the cache. If the new scope has no cache yet, it correctly falls through to the cold path. The risk is specifically when the same (dateRange, profileId) combination returns stale data after a rebuild or import has invalidated the backend truth -- the cache check passes, stale data renders, and only the background force=true refresh corrects it. The doc contract says 'route switching must discard expired requests' and 'switching scope must clear old cards first'.
findings.impact: Users could briefly see intelligence data from a previous analysis snapshot after a rebuild or import event, creating a trust issue. The feature doc explicitly says switching scope must clear old evidence before showing new results.
findings.suggestedFix: When the scope key changes in the hook, clear the previous scope from the overview cache before checking for warm cache. Alternatively, add a generation counter or version stamp from the backend rebuild report so the client can detect that cached data is stale relative to the latest rebuild.

findings.title: Fallback copy tables in copy.ts duplicate the i18n catalog and will drift
findings.description: The intelligenceFallbackCopy and commonHealthFallbackCopy objects in copy.ts contain full three-language translation tables for 11+ keys each. These serve as a repair layer when the i18n catalog translator returns a raw key path. While the defensive intent is sound, maintaining two parallel sources of the same copy (the JSON catalog and these fallback objects) creates a drift risk. If a catalog key is renamed or its text is updated, the fallback silently masks the regression by returning the hardcoded string, making the bug invisible until someone reads the fallback code.
findings.impact: Silent copy drift: a catalog rename or text update would be masked by the fallback, producing inconsistent translations across the app. The translationLooksRaw check also uses namespace-cased comparison which could false-negative on edge cases.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider a test that verifies all fallback keys exist in the canonical catalog and match (or at least a lint rule). Alternatively, move toward a single source of truth by using the catalog directly and removing the fallback layer once catalog reliability is proven.

findings.title: cachedPrimarySectionForProfile and cachedSecondarySectionForDateRange iterate all cache entries
findings.description: These two functions iterate over all entries in the overviewCache Map, parsing JSON keys for each entry, to find a matching profile or date range. Combined with the unbounded cache growth issue, this becomes an O(N) scan that runs for every peek* call that falls through to the profile/date-range secondary lookup. JSON.parse on every cache key during a hot render path is allocation-heavy.
findings.impact: In an extended session with many cached scopes, peek calls for profile-scoped data (like interrupted habits) or date-range-scoped data (like multi-browser diff) pay a growing O(N) cost. This could contribute to jank during rapid scope transitions.
findings.suggestedFix: Maintain a secondary index Map keyed by profileId (for profile lookups) and by dateRange key (for date-range lookups) alongside the primary scope-keyed cache. Alternatively, parse and store the key components when writing to the cache.

findings.title: Assistant page exposes live AI interaction controls when optionalAiFeaturesAvailable is true but no provider works
findings.description: The assistant page has a correct deferred state when optionalAiFeaturesAvailable=false (current v0.2). However, lines 354-416 show a third disabled state for when AI is enabled but not configured (enabled=false or assistantEnabled=false). This path still renders suggestedQuestions as clickable-looking rows (lines 402-410) inside a div with className='result-row' that has interactive styling, even though no send action is possible. The doc says 'v0.2.0 amendment: Assistant route must display disabled / Coming in v0.3 state; must not provide seeded prompts'. While the current code gates correctly via optionalAiFeaturesAvailable=false, when v0.3 re-enables AI, the disabled-but-AI-configured branch will show seeded prompts without a working send mechanism.
findings.impact: When v0.3 re-enables AI features, users with AI enabled but assistant specifically disabled will see suggestion cards that look interactive but do nothing -- confusing UX. The current gate is correct but the inner disabled state needs cleanup.
findings.suggestedFix: When AI is enabled but assistant is disabled, do not render suggestedQuestions. Instead show only the StatusCallout with a direct Settings link to enable the assistant.

findings.title: useAsyncData hook silently swallows errors when cached data exists
findings.description: In the error handler of useAsyncData (line 163-166), if prev.data is not null (i.e., cached data existed), the error is silently set to null instead of being propagated. This means a fetch failure is invisible to the user as long as stale cached data exists. The UI will continue showing old data without any indication that a refresh failed. This design trades error visibility for resilience, but it means users could be making decisions on stale intelligence data without knowing the latest refresh failed.
findings.impact: Users see stale intelligence data after a backend error without any visual indicator that the data may be outdated. This conflicts with the Trust and Transparency principle -- the user should know when displayed data could not be refreshed.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider adding a 'stale' or 'refreshFailed' flag to the state so UI components can optionally show a subtle indicator (e.g., a warning badge on section metadata) that the displayed data could not be refreshed. This preserves the resilient UX while maintaining transparency.

findings.title: Overview seed cache uses hardcoded limits that must match consumer defaults exactly
findings.description: The seedPrimaryOverviewReadCache function seeds the section read cache with hardcoded parameters (e.g., sortBy: 'visit_count', limit: 40 for top sites, limit: 5 for refind pages, page: 0, pageSize: 10 for query families, limit: 50 for search concepts). These magic numbers must exactly match the default parameters used by the overview-read-primary.ts getTopSites/getRefindPages/etc. functions and also the sections.tsx consumer defaults. If any consumer changes its default (e.g., requesting 6 refind pages instead of 5), the cache seed will not match and a redundant IPC call will be made. There is no centralized constant or type contract enforcing this alignment.
findings.impact: Drift between overview seed defaults and consumer defaults silently degrades the cache hit rate, causing redundant backend round trips without any visible error.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract the default parameters into named constants (e.g., DEFAULT_TOP_SITES_LIMIT = 40, DEFAULT_REFIND_LIMIT = 5) shared between the overview seeder and the section readers. This makes the contract explicit and prevents drift.

findings.title: readRequestKey uses JSON.stringify for cache keys, creating allocation pressure on hot paths
findings.description: Every cache read, write, and peek call invokes JSON.stringify to compute the cache key. For peek* calls that happen during render (e.g., the secondary grid isReady checks in sections.tsx evaluate peek* for every slot on every render), this creates transient string allocations that the GC must collect. While individually fast, the aggregate cost during staged overview renders with 12+ secondary slots could be measurable.
findings.impact: Minor GC pressure during intelligence page renders. Not a blocking performance concern but contributes to allocation churn during the staged rendering pipeline.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider a lightweight key builder that concatenates strings with a delimiter instead of JSON.stringify, or memoize the key at the call site for peek* calls that run on every render.

findings.title: InsightAccessStrip domain input lacks validation feedback for non-domain input
findings.description: The Insight Access strip lets users type a domain and navigate to domain insights. The button is disabled only when domainValue.trim() is empty, but there is no validation that the entered string is actually a valid domain. A user could type 'hello world' and be navigated to /intelligence/domain/hello%20world, which would show an empty domain insights page. There is also no feedback when the entered domain has no data in the archive.
findings.impact: Users who mistype or enter non-domain text get a confusing empty page instead of helpful feedback. Minor but degrades the editorial intelligence surface polish.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a lightweight client-side domain format check (e.g., must contain at least one dot and no spaces) and disable the button when the input does not look like a domain. Consider also showing a 'no data for this domain' message on the domain-deep-dive route when results are empty.

findings.title: PaperIntelligencePanel renders conditionally on layout=paper query param alongside main sections
findings.description: The intelligence page renders PaperIntelligencePanel when ?layout=paper is present, but also always renders the main IntelligenceSections below it. This means in paper layout mode, users see both the paper panel AND the full sections grid, which is a confusing dual-surface experience. Similarly, the assistant page has the same pattern (line 468-507) where both PaperAssistantPanel and the regular conversation panel are conditionally rendered based on ?layout=paper. The paper layout appears to be a transitional design surface that was not fully exclusive-or'd against the legacy layout.
findings.impact: Users who discover the ?layout=paper parameter see a partially duplicated UI. While this is a dev-facing concern today, it creates confusion if the parameter leaks into production URLs.
findings.suggestedFix: Make the paper layout exclusive by wrapping the main sections in an else branch, or remove the ?layout=paper escape hatch if the v0.3 paper redesign is the canonical surface.

commendations.title: Excellent staged overview loading architecture
commendations.description: The useStagedIntelligenceOverview hook implements a sophisticated three-phase loading pattern (warm cache immediate render, primary batch first, secondary batch on idle) that directly addresses the documented ban on fan-out IPC requests. The integration of requestIdleCallback with RAF scheduling, the cancelled flag for cleanup, and the stale-while-revalidate pattern on revisit are all well-engineered for the 14.4M records performance target.

commendations.title: Principled AI feature deferral with honest disabled states
commendations.description: The release-capabilities.ts module provides clean, side-effect-free boolean flags that gate all optional AI features. The assistant page correctly shows differentiated disabled states (archive not initialized, encrypted/locked, AI deferred to v0.3, AI enabled but assistant disabled) with appropriate actions for each. The separation of the optionalAiFeaturesAvailable flag from the per-feature enabled checks creates a correct two-level gate that matches the ADR-009 v0.2.0 amendment exactly.

commendations.title: Well-decomposed secondary section heuristics with documented filtering rationale
commendations.description: The heuristics.ts module cleanly separates the decision of whether a secondary card is worth showing (hasMeaningfulStableSources, isMeaningfulFrictionSignal, isSearchBackedReopenedInvestigation, isMeaningfulPathFlow) from the sections that render them. Each filter has a clear JSDoc explaining what noise it prevents and what evidence threshold it requires. The normalizeFlowStep function even handles domain aliasing (twitter.com/x.com, chat.openai.com/chatgpt.com) correctly, showing attention to real-world browsing patterns.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Overview cache invalidation after backup/import may race with route rendering
crossModuleConcerns.description: The import page (src/pages/import/index.tsx line 383) calls clearIntelligenceOverviewCache() after a successful import. If the user navigates to /intelligence before the cache is cleared (or if the import completes in a background tab), the intelligence route may serve stale pre-import data from the warm cache. The Settings derived-state hooks also clear the cache on rebuild/clear, but there is no event-driven invalidation mechanism -- it relies on the clearing code running before the next intelligence route mount.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-IMPORT (import page cache clearing), FA-SETTINGS (derived-state rebuild hooks), FA-SHELL (shell-level data refresh lifecycle)

crossModuleConcerns.title: Shell-level runtimeStatus polling source should be checked for deduplication across routes
crossModuleConcerns.description: The intelligence runtime digest reads runtimeStatus from useShellData(), which the docs say must be a shared shell-level polling source. The assistant page also reads runtimeStatus from useShellData(). Other review focus areas should verify that the shell's refreshRuntimeStatus is not triggered redundantly when multiple intelligence-adjacent routes are mounted (e.g., sidebar + route + dashboard card all polling the same status endpoint).
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-SHELL (runtime status polling), FA-JOBS (jobs page runtime status consumption)

crossModuleConcerns.title: Profile scope honesty across intelligence and explorer deep links
crossModuleConcerns.description: The intelligence route correctly passes effectiveProfileId through all entity hrefs (domainHref, queryFamilyHref, refindHref, trailHref, dayHref). However, the doc contract says deep-links from scoped intelligence views must preserve profileId into Explorer. Other focus areas should verify that the evidenceHref helper and Explorer route honor the profileId query parameter when arriving from intelligence CTAs.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-EXPLORER (Explorer profileId query param handling), FA-RECALL (search/recall profile scope preservation)

summary: Reviewed 85 files across src/pages/settings/ (40 files), src/pages/audit/ (11 files), src/pages/security/ (7 files), and src/pages/schedule/ (7 files), totaling approximately 25,480 lines. The trust surfaces are generally well-architected with clean separation between workflow hooks (use-*-workflow.ts) and render-only panels. i18n coverage is strong, PME flows are explicit, and destructive actions follow the preview-then-execute grammar. Key concerns include: schedule/index.tsx exceeding the 1200-line maintainability threshold at 1308 lines with all state panels inlined; hardcoded English strings used for backend-originated comparison matching in app-lock and retention localization; the rekey "confirm" gate using an English-only comparison; and the audit chain-viz rendering all runs without virtualization.

findings.title: schedule/index.tsx at 1308 lines exceeds 1200-line review threshold
findings.description: The schedule route file is at 1308 lines, exceeding the 1200-line maintainability threshold defined in AGENTS.md. It inlines all five state-panel components (NotInstalledState, InstalledOkState, InstalledWarnState, InstalledErrorState) plus helper components (ManualMode, ScheduleSummary, IssueList, etc.) in one file. AGENTS.md requires adding a maintainability analysis task to BACKLOG.md for files exceeding 1200 lines.
findings.impact: This is already tracked as WORK-SCHEDULE-PAGE-MAINT-A in BACKLOG.md (blocked status). However, the file has grown from the 1296 lines noted in that entry to 1308 lines, indicating continued growth despite the freeze. New features or states will push it further past the 1400-line hard limit where only bug fixes are allowed.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract each state panel (NotInstalledState, InstalledOkState, InstalledWarnState, InstalledErrorState) into separate owner files under schedule/states/. The ManualMode, ScheduleSummary, IssueList, and VerificationList helper components could also be extracted into schedule/components/. This is already captured in the blocked WORK-SCHEDULE-PAGE-MAINT-A backlog item.

findings.title: Hardcoded English strings for backend degradation note matching in App Lock
findings.description: The App Lock section matches degradation notes from the backend using hardcoded English string comparisons to decide whether to localize them. The code compares note values against full English sentences ('App Lock only protects the PathKeep UI session...', 'Touch ID is available on this Mac...') and only localizes those exact matches. Any backend note that does not exactly match these two strings is rendered raw to the user. This violates the i18n shipping contract which requires all user-visible copy to be delivered in en/zh-CN/zh-TW.
findings.impact: Users running PathKeep in zh-CN or zh-TW will see raw English backend strings for any degradation notes that do not match the two hardcoded patterns. If the backend wording changes even slightly, localization breaks silently for all three locales.
findings.suggestedFix: Follow the same pattern used in security/helpers.ts (localizeSecurityWarning): create a dedicated localizeAppLockNote helper that matches known backend strings via stable substrings or typed codes rather than exact full-sentence equality. Better yet, request typed codes from the backend instead of relying on transport English text as the matching key.
findings.codeSnippet:
const localizedNote =
  note === 'App Lock only protects the PathKeep UI session. Archive encryption still protects data at rest.'
    ? t('settings.appLockBoundaryBody')
    : note === 'Touch ID is available on this Mac and can unlock the current PathKeep session.'
      ? t('settings.appLockTouchIdAvailable')
      : note  // <-- raw English falls through

findings.title: Hardcoded English strings for backend retention warning matching
findings.description: The retention section uses the same fragile pattern as app-lock: matching full English backend warning sentences to decide localization. Two exact-match cases are handled ('Pruning snapshots removes...' and 'Export pruning only...'), but any unmatched warning string passes through raw. This is the same anti-pattern as the App Lock section but in a different trust surface.
findings.impact: Unknown or modified backend retention warnings will display as raw English in all locales. The retention surface is a PME-sensitive area where users make destructive decisions about pruning data; unclear copy in the user's native language undermines the trust contract.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract a localizeRetentionWarning helper following the security/helpers.ts pattern. Use substring matching as a fallback for each known pattern, and log/surface unknown warnings explicitly rather than passing them through silently.
findings.codeSnippet:
const localizedWarning =
  warning === 'Pruning snapshots removes saved restore checkpoints...'
    ? t('settings.retentionSnapshotPruneWarning')
    : warning === 'Export pruning only removes local files...'
      ? t('settings.retentionExportPruneWarning')
      : warning  // <-- raw English falls through

findings.title: Rekey plaintext confirmation gate uses hardcoded English 'confirm' comparison
findings.description: The SecurityRekeyPanel disables the execute button until the user types 'confirm' when switching to plaintext mode. The comparison is hardcoded as `rekeyConfirmText !== 'confirm'`, and the placeholder is also 'confirm' in all three locales (checked the i18n catalog -- rekeyConfirmPlaceholder is 'confirm' for all three). While the placeholder being the same in all locales is technically consistent, the word 'confirm' is English and may confuse zh-CN/zh-TW users who do not read English.
findings.impact: Chinese-speaking users may not understand they need to type the English word 'confirm' to proceed with a critical security operation. This is a trust-surface usability issue.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider adding a localized explanation above the input that explicitly tells the user what word to type. Alternatively, use a locale-specific confirmation word (though this adds complexity). At minimum, the body text near the input should explain in the user's language what they need to type and why.

findings.title: Schedule status bar renders raw backend status.label without localization
findings.description: The ScheduleStatusBar and ScheduleSummary both render `status.label` directly into the UI. This label comes from the backend (e.g., the LaunchAgent label like 'com.yi-ting.pathkeep.backup') and is a technical identifier, not user-facing copy. While it is rendered in a mono-styled span (appropriate for technical identifiers), it is displayed alongside localized text without any explanatory wrapper.
findings.impact: Users see a raw technical identifier (e.g., 'com.yi-ting.pathkeep.backup') without context about what it means. The identifier is appropriate as a technical evidence value per the design guidelines (monospace for path/ID/command), but a label prefix like 'Scheduler label:' should precede it for clarity.
findings.suggestedFix: In the ScheduleStatusBar (line 319), the label is already in a mono span. The ScheduleSummary (line 898) correctly pairs it with t('schedule.label'). Consider whether the status bar needs additional context, or if the mono style is sufficient given it appears alongside the state badge.

findings.title: Audit chain-viz renders all runs without pagination or virtualization
findings.description: The audit page renders all filteredRuns in a single chain-viz div. For a 14.4M record archive with 60 years of daily backups, this could mean ~22,000 backup runs rendered as DOM nodes simultaneously. The chain visualization creates a button + metadata div for each run, plus chain-link arrow dividers between them. There is no pagination, virtualization, or max-visible-runs cap.
findings.impact: With the 14.4M record design ceiling and daily backups over decades, the audit page could render tens of thousands of chain blocks, causing significant DOM pressure and slow initial render. However, this is mitigated by the fact that recentRuns likely comes from the shell snapshot which presumably limits the returned count on the backend side.
findings.suggestedFix: Verify that the backend limits recentRuns to a reasonable count (e.g., last 100-500 runs). If the backend already caps this, add a comment documenting the contract. If not, either add backend pagination for the audit run list, or add a client-side 'show more' pattern with an initial cap of ~100 runs.

findings.title: Security unlock panel always shows keyring buttons even when archive is plaintext
findings.description: The SecurityUnlockPanel always renders the 'Store in Keyring' and 'Clear Keyring' buttons regardless of whether the archive is encrypted or plaintext. When the archive is plaintext, storing a database key in the keyring is meaningless since there is no key to store. The conditional rendering only gates the unlock/lock buttons (lines 264-295), but the keyring buttons at lines 296-313 are always visible.
findings.impact: Users with plaintext archives see keyring management buttons that serve no practical purpose. Clicking 'Store in Keyring' with a plaintext archive and an empty key field would either error or store an empty value. This creates confusion about what the keyring does in the plaintext context.
findings.suggestedFix: Wrap the 'Store in Keyring' and 'Clear Keyring' buttons in a conditional that checks `status.encrypted`. Alternatively, show them disabled with an explanation that keyring management is only relevant for encrypted archives.

findings.title: Security unlock panel uses busy text as button label instead of spinner/disabled pattern
findings.description: The unlock, keyring, and rekey buttons use the pattern `busy === t('security.unlockArchive') ? busy : t('security.unlockArchive')`, which compares the busy label against the translated button text. When busy matches, the button shows the same text (the action name). This means (a) the button text does not change during the operation -- it stays the same label, and (b) the comparison relies on the busy string exactly matching the translation, which is fragile if translations change.
findings.impact: Users get no visual feedback that a button action is in progress beyond the BusyOverlay rendered at the route level. The button itself does not appear disabled or show any loading state. The comparison pattern is also fragile against i18n key changes.
findings.suggestedFix: Disable the buttons when `busy` is non-null (regardless of which action is busy), and optionally change the label to a loading indicator. The current pattern where `busy === t('...')` compares translated strings is unnecessarily fragile.

findings.title: Settings and Audit pages maintain dual layout paths (paper vs. non-paper)
findings.description: Settings and Audit pages still have `?layout=paper` conditional rendering. Settings shows PaperSettingsHeader vs SettingsSectionNav and an overview block. Audit conditionally renders PaperAuditPanel. Per the BACKLOG, the v0.2 legacy layout was retired in the Explorer (WORK-V03-LEGACY-RETIRE), but Settings and Audit still maintain dual paths. This creates maintenance burden and potential for the two paths to drift.
findings.impact: Two rendering paths must be maintained and tested for both Settings and Audit. If paper is the intended direction, the legacy path is dead code. If both are needed, tests must cover both.
findings.suggestedFix: Evaluate whether the non-paper path in Settings and the paper conditional in Audit are still needed. If the v0.3 paper redesign is the canonical direction, consider retiring the dual paths as was done for Explorer. If both are needed, document why.

findings.title: Data migration section uses manual .replace() chains instead of i18n interpolation
findings.description: The DataMigrationSection uses chained .replace() calls to interpolate values into translated strings: `.replace('{path}', ...).replace('{size}', ...).replace('{fileCount}', ...)`. The i18n system supports interpolation via the second argument to `t()`, e.g., `t('key', { path, size, fileCount })`. The manual .replace() pattern is fragile (fails silently if the placeholder is missing from the translation) and inconsistent with how other components use the i18n system.
findings.impact: If a translator omits or renames a placeholder like '{path}', the raw placeholder text appears to the user instead of failing loudly. The rest of the codebase consistently uses t() with variables.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace the .replace() chains with proper i18n interpolation: `t('settings.migrationExportedBody', { path: phase.result.bundlePath, size: formatBytes(phase.result.bytesWritten), fileCount: phase.result.manifest.files.length })`.
findings.codeSnippet:
// Current fragile pattern:
t('settings.migrationExportedBody')
  .replace('{path}', phase.result.bundlePath)
  .replace('{size}', formatBytes(phase.result.bytesWritten))
  .replace('{fileCount}', String(phase.result.manifest.files.length))

findings.title: Audit filter options are derived from detailCache with O(n) iteration per filter dimension
findings.description: The audit page derives profileOptions, runTypeOptions, sourceOptions, and artifactOptions each from separate useMemo hooks that iterate over the detailCache or indexedRuns. Additionally, healthScopeRuns duplicates a large part of the filteredRuns logic. Each of these five useMemo blocks iterates the full runs list, and the filter/health computations together iterate the list 7+ times per render cycle.
findings.impact: With a small number of runs (typical shell snapshot size), this is negligible. But the pattern would not scale well if the audit page ever needed to support unbounded run history. The duplicated filter logic between filteredRuns and healthScopeRuns is also a maintenance risk -- any filter change must be applied in both places.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider a single-pass derivation that computes all filter option sets, severity counts, and filtered results in one iteration. Extract the shared filter predicate logic into a helper to avoid the duplication between filteredRuns and healthScopeRuns.

findings.title: Security rekey does not validate key strength or show password strength indicator
findings.description: The handleExecuteRekey function only checks if the new key is non-empty when encrypting (`!request.newKey?.trim()`). There is no minimum length requirement, no password strength indicator, and no confirmation field for the new encryption key. Given that password loss equals data loss (per archive.md section 5), the lack of any password quality guidance is a trust surface gap.
findings.impact: Users can set a single-character encryption password for their entire archive. Given the 'password loss = data loss' warning, the absence of any strength guidance leaves users vulnerable to choosing weak passwords for critical data protection.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a minimum key length requirement (e.g., 8 characters) and a visual password strength indicator. Consider adding a 'confirm new key' field to prevent typos. The archive.md feature spec explicitly calls for 'clear, prominent warning' about password security, and a strength indicator serves that goal.

commendations.title: Excellent workflow hook separation across all trust surfaces
commendations.description: The consistent extraction of workflow logic into dedicated hooks (use-security-workflow.ts, use-schedule-workflow.ts, use-settings-route-state.ts, use-audit-data.ts) is exemplary. Each hook owns the async state machine, mutations, and error handling while the route component remains a pure composition shell. This pattern makes the trust surfaces testable in isolation and prevents business logic from leaking into render code. The pattern is consistent across all four routes in scope.

commendations.title: Thorough PME flow implementation in Security rekey and Data Migration
commendations.description: The Security rekey flow (preview -> confirmation gate -> execute) and Data Migration import flow (file picker -> preview panel with manifest/schema/overwrite details -> explicit confirm -> result) both faithfully implement the PME (Preview -> Manual -> Execute) grammar. The preview surfaces show concrete details (snapshot paths, schema versions, file counts, migration lists, exclusion notes) rather than vague summaries. The data migration section even handles encrypted bundle source-key prompts with typed error classification (sourceKeyRequired/sourceKeyInvalid/generic). This is exactly the level of transparency the design docs demand.

commendations.title: Consistent localization of backend warning strings via helper functions
commendations.description: The security/helpers.ts localizeSecurityWarning function demonstrates the right pattern for handling backend-originated English strings: explicit switch-case matching, substring fallback matching for minor wording variations, and transparent passthrough for unknown warnings. The helpers.ts localizeAiIntegrationPreview and localizeIntelligenceLocalHostPreview functions in settings extend this pattern comprehensively to cover dozens of backend strings. While some surfaces (app-lock, retention) have not adopted this pattern yet, the pattern itself is well-designed and should be the model for the remaining surfaces.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Backend degradation/warning strings use English transport text as matching keys
crossModuleConcerns.description: Multiple settings and security surfaces match backend-originated warning/note strings using exact or substring English text comparison. This is fragile and creates an implicit coupling between the Rust backend's warning text and the frontend localization layer. The root cause is that the backend emits freeform English strings rather than typed codes. This affects app-lock-section.tsx, retention-section.tsx, security/helpers.ts, and settings/helpers.ts (the latter handles it better with the localizeAiIntegrationLine approach). A cross-cutting solution would be to define typed warning/note codes on the Rust side and match on those codes in the frontend.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: src-tauri backend warning emission, src/lib/types.ts ScheduleIssue/SecurityStatus/RetentionPreview warning shapes, all Settings sub-sections that consume backend warnings

crossModuleConcerns.title: Audit chain-viz scalability depends on shell snapshot recentRuns cap
crossModuleConcerns.description: The audit page renders all snapshot.recentRuns as chain blocks without any client-side pagination. Whether this is a performance risk depends entirely on how many runs the backend includes in the AppSnapshot.recentRuns field. This cap lives in the shell-data-context and backend snapshot command, which are outside this review scope. The Explorer and Intelligence routes should be checked for similar unbounded rendering of backend collections.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: src/app/shell-data-context.tsx (recentRuns shape), src-tauri backend snapshot command (run limit), src/pages/dashboard/index.tsx (may also render recentRuns)

summary: Reviewed 36 files across src/pages/import/ (15 files), src/pages/jobs/ (7 files), src/pages/onboarding/ (15 files), and src/pages/trust-flows/ (6 files). The import wizard, onboarding flow, and jobs dashboard are well-structured with clean module boundaries, solid trust-flow test coverage, and good PME adherence. Key issues found: a wrong backend import path in security-step.tsx (uses legacy module), missing concurrent import guard in the UI, inconsistent i18n interpolation patterns in onboarding, no password strength validation during onboarding, and AI job handlers silently swallowing errors. The codebase shows strong adherence to the paper+archival aesthetic and the project's separation of concerns philosophy.

findings.title: security-step.tsx imports from legacy backend module instead of backend-client
findings.description: The security step imports `backend` from `../../lib/backend` (the legacy compatibility surface) instead of `../../lib/backend-client` (the canonical backend client). AGENTS.md explicitly states: 'src/lib/backend.ts is legacy / browser-preview fixture surface, not to be used for new shell contract.' Every other file in the import and onboarding scope uses `../../lib/backend-client`. While both modules expose a `keyringStatus()` method, the legacy module is documented as a deprecated surface and the two implementations may diverge in behavior or typing over time.
findings.codeSnippet: import { backend } from '../../lib/backend'
findings.impact: Currently functional because both modules expose `keyringStatus()`, but the legacy module is not the source of truth and may be removed or drift. AGENTS.md forbids new usage of this module.
findings.suggestedFix: Change the import to `import { backend } from '../../lib/backend-client'` in both security-step.tsx and security-step.test.tsx.

findings.title: Import confirm button not disabled when an archive-write task is already running
findings.description: The import route correctly detects `activeImportTask` from `archiveTasks` (line 104-109), and passes it to the wizard panel for progress display. However, the 'Confirm Import' button in wizard-panel.tsx is only disabled by the local `importing` boolean state -- it does not check whether `activeImportTask` already exists. archive.md explicitly requires: 'When an archive-write task is already running, a second import/backup action must connect to the existing task and notify the user, rather than starting a parallel write.' A user could start a second import while a prior one is still in progress if they navigate away and back, or if `importing` was reset after a partial failure.
findings.codeSnippet: disabled={importing || willImportFileCount === 0}
findings.impact: Risk of concurrent archive writes from the UI layer. The backend may have its own serialization guard, but the archive spec requires the UI to prevent this proactively.
findings.suggestedFix: Add `importTask` to the disabled condition on the confirm button: `disabled={importing || willImportFileCount === 0 || importTask !== null}`. Also show a StatusCallout when `importTask` is present on the select step, linking to /jobs.

findings.title: AI job retry and cancel handlers silently swallow errors
findings.description: The `handleReplayAiJob` and `handleCancelAiJob` functions have no `catch` block. If `backend.replayAiJob()` or `backend.cancelAiJob()` throws, the error propagates unhandled to the `finally` block (which only clears the action state), and the user sees no feedback about the failure. Compare with `handleRetryRuntimeJob` and `handleCancelRuntimeJob` directly below, which do have `catch` blocks that call `setPageError(message)`. This is an inconsistent error-handling pattern where AI job mutations fail silently while runtime job mutations surface errors.
findings.impact: Users clicking 'Retry' or 'Cancel' on a failed AI job get no feedback if the operation fails. The error is silently swallowed and the UI resets to its prior state.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a `catch` block to both handlers matching the pattern used by `handleRetryRuntimeJob`: catch the error, call `describeError()`, and set `setPageError(message)` so the user sees feedback.

findings.title: No password minimum length or strength validation during onboarding
findings.description: The onboarding security step only validates that the password is non-empty (`.trim()`) and that the confirmation matches. There is no minimum length, no strength feedback, and no entropy check. archive.md says 'password loss equals data loss' and requires 'prominent, clear warning requiring users to keep passwords safe.' The BACKLOG item WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A already notes that the App Lock KDF needs strengthening including 'increased minimum entropy,' but the archive encryption password during onboarding has the same weakness -- a single-character password is accepted for database encryption.
findings.impact: Users can encrypt their entire archive with a trivially weak password (e.g., a single space character), creating a false sense of security for data they intend to keep for decades.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a minimum password length check (e.g., 8 characters) in `handleSecurityContinue` and `handleFinish`. Add a visual strength indicator on the security step. This is partially tracked by BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A but the onboarding-specific enforcement is not explicitly called out there.

findings.title: Onboarding uses manual .replace() for i18n interpolation while import uses t() vars parameter
findings.description: Onboarding step files use manual `.replace('{count}', ...)` string interpolation in 9+ places (browser-detection-step.tsx lines 82-83, 93, 129; ready-step.tsx lines 71-73, 147, 211, 217; schedule-step.tsx line 73). Meanwhile, all import route files consistently use the i18n system's built-in vars parameter `t('key', { count: value })`. This inconsistency means onboarding is fragile to multi-occurrence placeholders (`.replace()` only replaces the first occurrence), does not benefit from any future i18n framework features (pluralization rules, etc.), and creates a maintenance inconsistency across sibling routes.
findings.impact: Inconsistent pattern across sibling routes; fragile to i18n evolution and multi-occurrence placeholder bugs.
findings.suggestedFix: Refactor the onboarding .replace() calls to use the `t('key', { count, selected })` vars parameter pattern that the import route already uses consistently.

findings.title: Duplicated Full Disk Access error detection logic across import and onboarding
findings.description: The function `isFullDiskAccessError()` in import/index.tsx and `formatOnboardingError()` in onboarding/index.tsx both contain hardcoded i18n string matching to detect Full Disk Access errors from the backend (`'Full Disk Access'`, `'完全磁盘访问权限'`, `'完整磁碟取用權'`). These are duplicated across two route owners and rely on matching backend error message substrings in three languages. If the backend changes its error wording or adds a new locale, both places must be updated. Additionally, onboarding has a slightly different set of strings (includes `'Safari History.db'`). This is a fragile pattern -- ideally the backend would return a typed error code rather than requiring UI-side string matching.
findings.impact: Maintenance burden: changes to backend error messages silently break the UI Full Disk Access action surfacing. Onboarding has an extra check for '全盤讀取權限' vs import which has it, but import also checks for it while onboarding does not -- the sets are inconsistent.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract a shared `isFullDiskAccessError()` helper into a platform-guidance utility and have both routes import it. Better yet, have the backend return a typed error code (e.g., `{ code: 'FULL_DISK_ACCESS_REQUIRED' }`) that the frontend can match without string comparisons.

findings.title: normalizedOptionalCount treats zero as null, hiding legitimate zero-progress states
