findings.description: The `normalizedOptionalCount` helper returns null when the value is 0 (`value > 0` check). This means a genuine progress event with `processedRecords: 0` (beginning of import) is treated identically to `processedRecords: null` (no data available). The archive spec says: 'UI must not pretend it is at 0 / 1 or 0% -- show indeterminate progress.' But by normalizing 0 to null, the code cannot distinguish between 'zero records processed so far' (determinate: show 0/N) and 'count not available' (indeterminate). In practice, this means the very beginning of an import always shows the fallback file-level progress instead of record-level progress, even when record counts are being tracked.
findings.impact: Minor UX regression at the start of imports -- record-level progress is hidden until the first record is processed. The impact is small because the transition is typically fast.
findings.suggestedFix: Change `value > 0` to `value >= 0` so that zero is treated as a legitimate count, or use a sentinel value / separate boolean to distinguish 'tracking not available' from 'tracking started at zero.'

findings.title: Dead paper layout gate behind query parameter in import route
findings.description: The import route conditionally renders `PaperImportPanel` only when `searchParams.get('layout') === 'paper'`. The BACKLOG item WORK-V03-LEGACY-RETIRE confirms that `?layout=legacy` was already retired from the explorer and the paper design is now the default. However, the import route still gates its paper panel behind an opt-in query parameter that is never set by any navigation link in the codebase (grep found zero references to `?layout=paper` anywhere else in src/). This means the PaperImportPanel component and its PaperImportView are effectively dead code in production -- they render only for manual URL manipulation.
findings.impact: Dead code path and unreachable component. The paper import panel was likely intended to be the new default but was never promoted from its feature-flag state.
findings.suggestedFix: Either make the paper import panel the default (remove the query param gate and render it unconditionally), or remove it if the design intent has changed. Consult the design docs to determine which.

findings.title: Onboarding schedule step does not re-fetch preview after interval change
findings.description: The schedule preview (schedulePlan and scheduleStatus) is fetched once when step === 4, with `[step, snapshot]` as the effect dependencies. When the user changes the backup interval via `onSelectDueAfterHours`, the config is updated but the schedule preview is not re-fetched -- it still shows the old interval's plan. The schedule plan includes platform-specific artifacts (LaunchAgent plist, Task Scheduler XML) whose content depends on the interval, so the preview becomes stale after an interval change.
findings.impact: Users see a schedule preview that does not reflect their newly selected interval. If they then click 'Install,' the backend will use the updated config, so the installed schedule is correct, but the preview was misleading.
findings.suggestedFix: Add `currentConfig.dueAfterHours` (or the entire config) to the effect's dependency array so the preview re-fetches when the interval changes. Alternatively, show a 'Preview outdated' notice after an interval change.

findings.title: Batch revert/restore uses native window.confirm instead of designed dialog
findings.description: The `handleBatchMutation` function uses `window.confirm()` for the revert/restore confirmation dialog. This is a native browser dialog that cannot be styled, does not match the paper+archival aesthetic, bypasses the app's i18n system for the dialog chrome (OK/Cancel buttons are in the OS language, not the app language), and would look out of place in the Tauri desktop shell.
findings.impact: Minor aesthetic and i18n inconsistency. The dialog content text itself is correctly translated via `t()`, but the native OK/Cancel buttons are not.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace `window.confirm()` with a custom confirmation dialog component that matches the paper design system and uses i18n'd button labels.

findings.title: Jobs page fires parallel refreshAppData + refreshRuntimeStatus on every action
findings.description: Every job mutation handler (handleRefresh, handlePauseChange, handleReplayAiJob, handleCancelAiJob, handleRetryRuntimeJob, handleCancelRuntimeJob) fires `Promise.all([refreshAppData(), refreshRuntimeStatus()])`. With 6 handlers, a rapid sequence of job actions (retry, cancel, pause) could generate a burst of redundant full-snapshot reads. The comment in the BACKLOG (WORK-PERF-RUNTIME-LITE-A) notes that `load_intelligence_runtime` takes ~2.9s on a 264k-row archive. Rapid consecutive calls would queue up expensive backend reads.
findings.impact: On large archives, rapid job mutations could create noticeable UI lag from stacked full-snapshot reads. This is not critical today but scales poorly with archive size.
findings.suggestedFix: Debounce or deduplicate the refresh calls so that rapid sequential mutations only trigger one refresh cycle. Consider using the planned lightweight runtime_digest path from WORK-PERF-RUNTIME-LITE-A.

commendations.title: Excellent module decomposition in the import route
commendations.description: The import route is cleanly split into focused modules: index.tsx (route orchestration, 520 lines), shared.ts (pure helpers and types, 614 lines), wizard-panel.tsx (wizard rendering), select-step.tsx (source selection), review-panels.tsx (follow-through review), workflow-panel.tsx (workflow explainer composition), and use-import-review-state.ts (review state hook). Each module has clear doc comments with Responsibilities / Not Responsible For / Dependencies / Performance Notes headers. This decomposition exactly follows the AGENTS.md constraint against god components and keeps every file well under the 800-line new-file limit.

commendations.title: Thorough trust-flow test coverage for the import workflow
commendations.description: The import-flows.test.tsx file at 1324 lines provides deep end-to-end coverage of the import wizard: preview/execute/revert/doctor-review in a translated locale (zh-CN), browser-profile detection and selection, file picker integration, Browser Direct vs Takeout routing, Safari Full Disk Access recovery flow, concurrent batch preview failure handling, progress overlay painting, and audit-path copy/open actions. The tests are realistic and exercise genuine failure modes (progress subscription failure, stale batch preview, settings pane failure), not just happy paths.

commendations.title: Clean onboarding step extraction with proper state ownership
commendations.description: The onboarding flow demonstrates good state management discipline: the route owner (index.tsx) holds all workflow validation and side effects, while each step renderer (welcome-step, browser-detection-step, storage-step, security-step, schedule-step, ready-step) is purely presentational. The security step even includes a proactive keyring availability probe on mount (useEffect that calls backend.keyringStatus) to prevent users from discovering keyring failures only at the final 'Create archive' step. The schedule preview is lazily loaded only when the user reaches step 4, avoiding unnecessary backend calls during onboarding.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Legacy backend.ts module still consumed by onboarding security step
crossModuleConcerns.description: security-step.tsx imports from the legacy `../../lib/backend` module. This is the only file in the import/jobs/onboarding scope that does so -- all others use `../../lib/backend-client`. The backend-client focus area should verify that no other routes still depend on the legacy module.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-SHELL (shell data context), FA-LIB (backend-client vs legacy backend migration)

crossModuleConcerns.title: Full Disk Access error detection duplicated across routes
crossModuleConcerns.description: Both import/index.tsx and onboarding/index.tsx contain hardcoded string matching for Full Disk Access backend errors in multiple languages. The explorer or settings areas may also need this pattern. This should be consolidated into a shared utility in the lib/ layer.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-SETTINGS, FA-EXPLORER (any route that handles Safari errors)

crossModuleConcerns.title: Shell task store and concurrent archive-write guard
crossModuleConcerns.description: The import route detects `activeImportTask` from shell tasks but does not use it to prevent concurrent imports. The shell/shell-tasks area should verify that the global task store enforces single-writer semantics at the shell level, since the import route does not enforce it in its own UI.

summary: Reviewed 18 files across the Dashboard route, annotation hooks (desktop and local), og-image hydration hook, backend-client layers, paper-detail-panel, and supporting test/i18n files. The dashboard and annotation subsystems are well-architected with clean module boundaries, honest data rendering, and good test coverage. Found two notable correctness issues (misleading "This Week" data labels, missing frontend annotation length enforcement), one meaningful UX concern (the stat-strip "GB" label), and several minor quality items including a duplicated helper function. i18n has full 3-locale parity for all dashboard and annotation copy.

findings.title: "This Week" card shows all-time totals, not actual weekly data
findings.description: DashboardThisWeek receives `totalPages` and `totalUrls` from the parent, which are `readyDashboard.totalVisits` and `readyDashboard.totalUrls` -- these are all-time archive-wide totals, not this-week data. The card is titled 'This week' (i18n key `dashboard.thisWeekTitle`) and shows a week badge, but the stats underneath are cumulative archive totals. The i18n label `weekStatPages` says 'Pages' and `weekStatUrls` says 'New URLs', implying these are weekly numbers. A user with 14.4M all-time visits would see '14.4M' under a 'This week' heading -- seriously misleading. Similarly `recentRunsCount` is `recentRuns.length` from the full dashboard snapshot, not filtered to this week.
findings.impact: Directly violates Trust & Transparency: the UI presents all-time numbers under a weekly heading. Users will make wrong conclusions about their recent browsing activity. This is exactly the kind of 'numbers that looked like real weekly data but weren't' that the heroMessage comment called out.
findings.suggestedFix: Either (a) rename the card to 'Archive overview' and change the stat labels to match all-time semantics, or (b) wire a real weekly query (e.g. using `dashboardThreadsRange(now)` to get visits from the last 7 days) and show actual this-week numbers. Option (a) is the honest minimal fix; option (b) is the proper feature implementation.

findings.title: Hero stat strip "Size" label is hardcoded to 'GB' regardless of actual unit
findings.description: The i18n key `statSize` is 'GB' in all three locales. The stat value comes from `humanizeBytes(totalStorageBytes)` which produces adaptive unit strings like '512 B', '2.0 KB', '5.0 MB', '3.0 GB'. When the archive is small (e.g. during early use), the value might read '5.0 MB' but the label beneath still says 'GB'. The label is a unit description, not a heading, so it actively misleads about what unit the number is in.
findings.impact: Users with small archives see contradictory information (value says MB, label says GB). The mismatch erodes trust in the data displayed.
findings.suggestedFix: Change the stat label to a generic heading like 'Size' / 'Archive' / 'Storage' in all three locales, since the value string from humanizeBytes already includes the correct unit suffix. Alternatively, extract the unit from humanizeBytes and use it as the label.

findings.title: No frontend enforcement of annotation notes (16 KiB) or tag (64 bytes, 64 count) limits
findings.description: Per docs/features/annotations.md section 3, the backend enforces hard limits: notes max 16 KiB after trim, tags max 64 bytes each, max 64 tags per URL. The frontend textarea has no maxLength attribute and no character counter warning as the user approaches 16 KiB. The tag input has no length validation. If a user types a very long note (unlikely but possible with paste), or adds 65+ tags, the write will silently fail on the backend and the optimistic cache will show data that was never persisted. The detail panel shows a char counter but no limit warning.
findings.impact: Users could write annotations they believe are saved but the backend rejects. The optimistic cache masks the rejection since the error handling on write failure in useDesktopAnnotations only updates lastError, and the panel only shows a generic 'notesSaveError' alert. The user has no idea their note was too long, or that they hit the tag limit.
findings.suggestedFix: Add `maxLength={16384}` to the textarea element. Add a visual warning when the char count approaches the limit (e.g. at 15000+). For tags, check `candidate.length > 64` before calling onUpdateTags, and check `tags.length >= 64` before allowing a new tag. Show the specific rejection reason from the backend error in the annotationError display.

findings.title: humanizeBytes is duplicated between dashboard-helpers.ts and archive-card.tsx
findings.description: archive-card.tsx defines a local `humanizeBytes` function (lines 100-110) that is functionally identical to the one exported from `dashboard-helpers.ts` (lines 87-96), except the local version returns '0 B' for zero input while the helpers version returns ''. The dashboard index.tsx imports from dashboard-helpers but archive-card.tsx uses its own copy.
findings.impact: Code duplication within the same feature area. The two implementations have subtly different zero-value behavior, which could cause inconsistent display if one is changed without the other.
findings.suggestedFix: Delete the local humanizeBytes in archive-card.tsx and import from dashboard-helpers.ts. Adjust the call site to handle the empty-string case: `humanizeBytes(bytes) || '0 B'`.

findings.title: useDesktopAnnotations cache grows unbounded across URL navigation
findings.description: The in-memory cache in `useDesktopAnnotations` is a `Record<string, Bundle>` that accumulates entries for every URL the user ever opens in the detail panel during a session. The `hydratedRef` and `locallyMutatedRef` sets also grow without bound. While the hook is per-URL on-demand (one URL at a time from the detail panel), a power user browsing through hundreds of records in a single session would accumulate hundreds of cache entries. Neither set nor the cache is ever pruned.
findings.impact: In a 14.4M record archive, a power user could theoretically browse enough URLs to accumulate significant memory in these data structures. Practically, this is unlikely to cause problems since each Bundle is tiny (a string + string array), but it violates the 'no unbounded memory growth' constraint.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider adding an LRU cap to the cache (e.g. keep the most recent 100 entries). Alternatively, document why this is acceptable given the per-URL detail panel interaction pattern makes it extremely unlikely anyone visits more than a few hundred URLs in a single session.

findings.title: useMemoGreeting is computed once per t reference and does not update if the dashboard stays mounted across hour boundaries
findings.description: useMemoGreeting uses `useMemo` with `[t]` as the dependency. Since the i18n translator reference `t` is stable across renders (it only changes on locale switch), the greeting is computed once when the dashboard mounts and never updates. If a user leaves the dashboard open from morning to afternoon, the greeting stays 'Good morning'. The year-heatmap card already has a midnight-crossing timer (localDayKey + schedule pattern) that refreshes at day boundaries. The greeting has no equivalent mechanism for hour boundaries.
findings.impact: Minor visual staleness. Most users will not notice since they rarely keep the dashboard tab open for hours, but it contrasts with the heatmap's careful midnight-crossing logic.
findings.suggestedFix: This is low priority. If addressed, either add an interval that checks `new Date().getHours()` every few minutes and updates a state var in the dependency array, or just accept this as a minor imperfection. It is not worth adding complexity for.

findings.title: On This Day card uses raw Date.toISOString().slice() instead of the isoDateOnly helper, causing timezone bugs
findings.description: The On This Day card computes `targetIso` via `targetDate.toISOString().slice(0, 10)`. This produces a UTC date string, which in negative-offset timezones (e.g. America/Phoenix UTC-7) can produce yesterday's date instead of today's. The year-heatmap-card.tsx explicitly handles this by parsing date parts as local Date components (lines 154-157) and the dashboard-helpers.ts module provides `isoDateOnly()` which correctly formats a Date in the local timezone. Using `.toISOString().slice(0, 10)` is the same timezone bug pattern the heatmap comment explicitly warns about.
findings.impact: In negative-offset timezones, the 'Jump to date' button in the On This Day header could link to the wrong date, off by one day. The backend query goes to yesterday instead of the intended one-year-ago date.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace `targetDate.toISOString().slice(0, 10)` with `isoDateOnly(targetDate)` from `./dashboard-helpers`. This aligns the On This Day card with the same local-timezone-safe pattern the heatmap card already uses.
findings.codeSnippet: const targetIso = targetDate.toISOString().slice(0, 10)

findings.title: Detail panel 'Saved - local' status does not distinguish between local-storage and desktop-backend persistence
findings.description: The detail panel shows `copy.notesSavedLocally` (which is 'Saved - local' in English) regardless of whether annotations are persisted to the Rust backend or to localStorage. The annotations.md doc section 4 explicitly calls this out as a known limitation: 'surface real backend save state when detail panel adjusts'. When the desktop hook is active, 'Saved - local' understates the actual persistence guarantee (it is in the canonical archive SQLite, not just localStorage). When the local hook is active, it overstates it (localStorage can be wiped by browser cleanup).
findings.impact: Minor trust messaging inconsistency. The user gets the same status text whether their notes are in a robust SQLite archive or volatile localStorage.
findings.suggestedFix: This is already tracked in annotations.md section 6 backlog as a follow-up. No action needed now, but worth noting it is a known gap.

findings.title: Active threads and year heatmap effects depend on t as a fetch trigger
findings.description: The useEffect dependency arrays for both the year-heatmap data fetch (line 147) and the active-threads data fetch (active-threads-card.tsx line 101) include `t` (the i18n translator). The translator reference changes on locale switch, which triggers a re-fetch. Since locale switches are rare in practice and the backend query is cheap, this is harmless, but it couples the data fetch lifecycle to an unrelated i18n concern. If the translator were ever made unstable (e.g. returning a new reference on every render), it would cause refetch storms.
findings.impact: No current impact. This is a code quality observation about coupling.
findings.suggestedFix: Remove `t` from the useEffect dependency arrays for data-fetching effects. The translator is not used in the fetch logic itself -- it is only used in the render output which re-renders naturally when `t` changes.

commendations.title: Excellent Trust & Transparency discipline in dashboard data rendering
commendations.description: The dashboard consistently follows the PathKeep principle of honest data display. Every card shows real backend data with proper loading/empty/error states. The heroMessage comment (dashboard.ts lines 104-109) explicitly documents the reasoning for removing fake substitutions. The On This Day card surfaces a transparent fallback note when LLM summaries are unavailable. No fabricated data anywhere.

commendations.title: Well-designed annotation hydration race guard prevents data loss
commendations.description: useDesktopAnnotations implements a clean race-condition guard (locallyMutatedRef) that prevents stale backend GET responses from overwriting user edits made while the hydration was in flight. This is tested explicitly in use-desktop-annotations.test.tsx. The pattern correctly prioritizes user intent over backend staleness. The optimistic cache + write-through architecture is simple and correct for the one-URL-at-a-time interaction pattern.

commendations.title: Clean module decomposition with clear responsibility boundaries
commendations.description: The dashboard route is well-decomposed into focused files: dashboard-helpers.ts (pure formatters), route-fallback-state.ts (pure state resolution), route-fallback-access.ts (archive access probe), route-fallback.tsx (fallback renderer), and dedicated card components. Each file has a clear doc header with Responsibilities / Not responsible for / Dependencies / Performance notes. The annotation system cleanly separates LocalAnnotations (interface) from useLocalAnnotations (localStorage) and useDesktopAnnotations (backend), enabling one-line swap at the route level.

commendations.title: Paper detail panel debounce-and-flush lifecycle handles edge cases correctly
commendations.description: The PaperDetailPanel's notes debounce system (lines 192-292) correctly handles unmount flush, entry-switch flush, and the nuanced case where the pending-flush closure captures the previous record's onUpdateNotes callback. The useLayoutEffect-based flush on entry switch ensures no data is lost when quickly navigating between records. The pendingFlushRef + saveTimer pattern avoids the common pitfall of losing edits on unmount.

crossModuleConcerns.title: og:image fetchMode config threading from Settings to Explorer
crossModuleConcerns.description: use-explorer-og-images.ts accepts a `fetchMode` prop (defaulting to 'background') and the Codex review finding C1 comment (line 161) mentions a data-sovereignty hole. The FA-SETTINGS review scope should verify that Settings link-previews-section correctly threads the user's fetch toggle through to this hook and that the backend refetch_og_images_impl also gates on the effective mode.

crossModuleConcerns.title: DashboardSnapshot earliestVisitAt/latestVisitAt population from Rust backend
crossModuleConcerns.description: The Dashboard Span stat depends on `readyDashboard.earliestVisitAt` and `readyDashboard.latestVisitAt`. These are optional fields on DashboardSnapshot. The FA-BACKEND review scope should verify these are correctly populated from the Rust `load_dashboard_snapshot` command and that they reflect the actual archive visit range, not just the most recent run's range.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-BACKEND (vault-core dashboard snapshot builder, vault-worker dashboard flow)

crossModuleConcerns.title: core-intelligence API module boundary for dashboard consumers
crossModuleConcerns.description: The dashboard route calls coreIntelligenceApi.getOnThisDay, getPathFlows, and getDiscoveryTrend directly. The FA-INTELLIGENCE review scope should verify these APIs handle edge cases (empty archive, profile-scoped queries with no matching data, concurrent calls) gracefully since the dashboard does not add defensive wrappers beyond its own try/catch.

focusArea: FA-ARCHIVE: Rust Archive Core + Intelligence Engine
summary: Reviewed 60+ Rust source files across archive/, intelligence/, and intelligence_runtime/ totaling approximately 34,000 lines. The archive ingest pipeline, source-evidence contract, and intelligence rebuild engine are well-architected with strong data integrity foundations. The code demonstrates careful attention to dedup correctness, bounded memory usage, incremental rebuild with fallback safety, and proper hot/cold storage separation. Key findings center on the 3,473-line tests.rs exceeding the 1,400-line hard limit (already tracked in BACKLOG), a fingerprint semantic mismatch that may silently re-hash Firefox/Safari visits using Chrome timestamp conventions, and several minor consistency and forward-compatibility observations. No SQL injection vulnerabilities were found -- all user input flows through parameterized queries. The intelligence pipeline phases are well-decomposed with clear ownership boundaries per submodule.

findings.title: visit_event_fingerprint uses Chrome-epoch timestamp for Firefox and Safari visits
findings.description: insert_visit computes event_fingerprint by calling unix_micros_to_chrome_time(visit.visit_time_ms * 1000) for ALL browser families (Chromium, Firefox, Safari), as the source_kind is hardcoded to 'chromium-history'. While the doc comment at line 220-240 explains this is intentional for dedup consistency, the visit_time value fed into the fingerprint hash is a Chrome-epoch microsecond timestamp derived from the Unix millisecond visit_time_ms. For Firefox and Safari, this converts a Unix millisecond timestamp into Chrome epoch space (adding 11644473600000000 microseconds) before hashing. This means the fingerprint encodes Chrome-specific time semantics for browsers that do not use Chrome epoch internally. If a future refactor changes the time conversion or if the same URL+time appears through a different ingest path (e.g., a future Firefox Takeout), the fingerprints would silently diverge. The dedup still works today because each browser family lands in distinct source_profiles, but the semantic mismatch creates fragile coupling between the fingerprint's internal representation and Chrome's timestamp epoch.
findings.impact: No immediate data loss since dedup is scoped per source_profile_id, but the Chrome-epoch encoding for non-Chrome visits creates a hidden semantic contract that will break if cross-family dedup is ever needed or if the time conversion path changes.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider making the fingerprint use the canonical Unix millisecond timestamp directly (visit.visit_time_ms) instead of converting to Chrome epoch first. Since the fingerprint is scoped per source_profile_id and cross-flow matching is explicitly not a contract (as documented), using the canonical timestamp would be both simpler and more semantically honest. This would require a one-shot migration of existing fingerprints or a version flag.
findings.codeSnippet:
visit_event_fingerprint(
    "chromium-history",
    &visit.url,
    unix_micros_to_chrome_time(visit.visit_time_ms.saturating_mul(1_000)),
    visit.title.as_deref(),
    visit.transition,
    visit.app_id.as_deref(),
)

findings.title: archive/tests.rs at 3,473 lines far exceeds the 1,400-line hard limit
findings.description: The archive regression test suite has grown to 3,473 lines, which is more than double the 1,400-line hard limit defined in AGENTS.md. Per project rules, files exceeding 1,400 lines should only receive bug fixes or minimal changes, with no new business logic. This file is a test file, so it does not contain business logic per se, but it still violates the spirit of the maintainability constraint -- understanding and navigating 3,473 lines of test code across mixed concerns (backup, import, rollback, doctor, export, retention, rekey, snapshot restore) is a significant maintenance burden.
findings.impact: High maintenance cost -- adding new regression tests requires navigating a 3,473-line file. Risk of test name collisions and unclear ownership boundaries. Already tracked as WORK-ARCHIVE-TEST-MAINT-A in BACKLOG.
findings.suggestedFix: Already tracked in BACKLOG as WORK-ARCHIVE-TEST-MAINT-A. The two-phase approach (audit then split) defined there is the right strategy. Priority should be elevated given the file has grown since the BACKLOG entry was created.

findings.title: visit_event_fingerprint uses JSON serialization + SHA-256 for every visit row
findings.description: visit_event_fingerprint constructs a serde_json::Value via the json! macro, serializes it to a String, then SHA-256 hashes the bytes. This involves heap allocation for the JSON object, string serialization, and a crypto hash -- all on the hot ingest path for every single visit. With 14.4M records, this is 14.4M JSON serializations + 14.4M SHA-256 hashes during a single large import. While SHA-256 itself is fast, the JSON serialization overhead (including the serde_json::Value intermediate) is non-trivial at scale.
findings.impact: Measurable but likely acceptable overhead on the import hot path. For a 14.4M record import, the JSON serialization allocations could add up to several seconds of wall time and significant GC pressure.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider using a cheaper fingerprint strategy: concatenate the fields with a fixed delimiter into a pre-allocated buffer, then hash that directly. This avoids the serde_json::Value allocation and JSON serialization entirely. Example: format!("{source_kind}|{url}|{visit_time}|{title}|{transition}|{app_id}") followed by sha256_hex. The current JSON approach has the advantage of deterministic field ordering, but a fixed-order format string achieves the same.

findings.title: LIST_HISTORY_SQL and COUNT_HISTORY_SQL are nearly identical 30-line WHERE clauses duplicated across 5 SQL constants
findings.description: The LIST_HISTORY_SQL and COUNT_HISTORY_SQL constants share an almost identical WHERE clause (14 advanced filter NOT EXISTS subqueries, profile/time/cursor conditions). The same pattern is duplicated again in history.rs as LIST_HISTORY_LEXICAL_SQL, LIST_HISTORY_FUZZY_CANDIDATES_SQL, and COUNT_HISTORY_LEXICAL_SQL. Any change to the advanced search filter contract (e.g., adding a new operator like 'has:og-image') must be replicated across all 5 SQL constants. The triple-duplication in mod.rs + history.rs creates a significant risk of filter drift between list and count queries.
findings.impact: Any new advanced search operator requires updating 5 SQL constants in lockstep. A missed update would cause count/list disagreement or filter bypass in one recall mode.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract the shared advanced-filter WHERE clause into a const or builder function that generates the filter fragment once, then compose it into the SELECT/COUNT/lexical/fuzzy SQL templates. This is a maintainability improvement, not a behavior change.

findings.title: source_evidence insert loops execute one INSERT per evidence row without statement caching
findings.description: persist_source_evidence iterates over all search/navigation/engagement/context evidence rows and native entities, executing one INSERT statement per row via transaction.execute(). Each execute() call re-prepares the statement internally. For a large Safari or Chromium profile with thousands of source-evidence rows per batch, this means thousands of individual SQLite statement preparations within the same transaction. While the transaction amortizes fsync cost, each execute still incurs statement preparation overhead.
findings.impact: For most backup profiles this is fast enough (hundreds of evidence rows). For Safari profiles with 3+ months of history or Chromium with rich context/navigation evidence, the per-row prepare+execute could become a bottleneck during the post-commit cold archive write.
findings.suggestedFix: Prepare the INSERT statements once outside the loop using connection.prepare(), then reuse the prepared statement for each row. This avoids repeated statement compilation. For even larger batches, consider multi-value INSERT syntax.

findings.title: url_last_visit_marker stores mixed-unit watermarks without documenting the unit per family
findings.description: url_last_visit_marker returns Chrome-epoch microseconds for Chromium profiles but raw Unix milliseconds for Firefox/Safari profiles. Both values are stored in the same profile_watermarks column (last_url_last_visit_time). The watermark is later used in incremental ingest to determine which URLs have changed (WHERE last_visit_time >= ?1). For Chromium this is compared against the source DB's native Chrome-epoch timestamp. For Firefox/Safari, the watermark stores a Unix millisecond value. This means the same watermark column stores fundamentally different units depending on the browser family, but the schema has no column indicating the unit.
findings.impact: Currently safe because each parser handles its own cursor semantics, but the mixed-unit watermark storage is a footgun for future parser changes or cross-family watermark comparison.
findings.suggestedFix: The comment already documents this as a known limitation and mentions tracking it separately for a one-shot migration. Consider adding a watermark_unit or watermark_family column to profile_watermarks to make the unit explicit, or normalize all watermarks to Unix milliseconds.

findings.title: intelligence_rebuild run_id uses Utc::now().timestamp_millis() instead of a proper sequence
findings.description: The intelligence rebuild run_id is generated as Utc::now().timestamp_millis(). This produces a unique-ish identifier but has two weaknesses: (1) two rebuilds started within the same millisecond would collide, and (2) the run_id has no structural relationship to the canonical archive runs table which uses autoincrement. The intelligence run_id is used for module runtime updates and checkpoint tracking, not for canonical run-ledger entries, so this is not a data integrity issue, but it could cause confusion when correlating intelligence rebuild history with archive run history.
findings.impact: Minimal -- the intelligence run_id is used only within the derived intelligence plane and does not participate in canonical archive runs.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider using a monotonic counter or UUID for intelligence run_id, or document clearly that this is a derived-plane-only identifier with no relation to the canonical runs table.

findings.title: intelligence/phase_three.rs at 1,091 lines approaches the review threshold
findings.description: phase_three.rs contains 1,091 lines. While it is under the 1,200-line review threshold, it is approaching it. The file owns 5 distinct read model entrypoints (breadth_index, habit_patterns, interrupted_habits, observed_interactions, path_flows), each with its own SQL and row-mapping logic. As more Phase 3 surfaces are added, this file will cross the threshold.
findings.impact: No immediate issue, but the file will need monitoring as new Phase 3 intelligence surfaces are added.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider splitting the 5 entrypoints into individual focused files (intelligence_breadth.rs, intelligence_habits.rs, intelligence_path_flows.rs, etc.) before adding more surfaces to this module.

findings.title: build_sessions and build_search_trails clone StructuralVisitRecord fields repeatedly
findings.description: build_sessions creates a new StructuralVisitRecord for each visit by cloning String fields (url, profile_id, registrable_domain, search_engine, search_query) twice -- once for the start_new check and once for push_visit. For 14.4M visits, this results in 28.8M string clones of URL and domain fields. The StructuralVisitRecord is only used as a temporary projection for the session builder and could use borrowed references instead.
findings.impact: Non-trivial allocation pressure during large rebuilds. The cloning of URL strings (which can be hundreds of bytes each) 28.8M times could add measurable overhead to the structural rebuild phase.
findings.suggestedFix: Refactor StructuralVisitRecord to borrow from VisitRecord using lifetime parameters, or pass the VisitRecord directly to the session builder since all needed fields are already present on VisitRecord.

commendations.title: Exemplary hot/cold source-evidence architecture with bounded-memory deferred payloads
commendations.description: The DeferredSourceEvidencePayload enum (InMemory / SpoolFile / ChunkFile) and the DeferredSourceEvidenceBuilder demonstrate excellent engineering for a product that must handle 14.4M records. Small payloads stay in memory, large ones spill to temp files under staging/, and the ChunkFile variant supports streaming line-delimited JSON for Safari imports with months of context evidence. This design keeps the canonical ingest hot path fast while ensuring source-native evidence is never lost, even under memory pressure. The 512KB threshold is well-chosen and the spill directory is properly managed.

commendations.title: Well-documented ingest dedup contract with comprehensive edge-case test coverage
commendations.description: The ingest pipeline has remarkably thorough documentation of its dedup semantics. The insert_visit fingerprint contract is documented with a 40-line block comment explaining why source_kind is hardcoded, why cross-flow fingerprint matching is not a contract, and what would break if the hardcode changed. The test fingerprint_is_family_agnostic_within_backup_writer explicitly pins this contract. The dedup_scenarios_* test modules (baselines, chromium_sidecars, edge_cases with 5 focused child modules, takeout) provide deep coverage of real-world ingest scenarios including unicode, empty DBs, concurrent access, and parser ordering. This is production-grade data integrity testing.

commendations.title: Intelligence rebuild pipeline with incremental/fallback/noop three-mode safety net
commendations.description: The intelligence rebuild engine implements a careful three-mode execution strategy for each stage: noop (watermark unchanged), incremental (append-only delta from last checkpoint), and fallback-full (clear + recompute). The fallback triggers are explicit and documented -- version change, watermark regression, delta mismatch. Each mode saves its own checkpoint and records its execution mode in the report, making post-hoc debugging straightforward. The batched fallback path (rebuild_visit_derived_facts_in_batches) uses bounded cursor-driven iteration instead of loading all visits into memory, which correctly handles the 14.4M-record scenario. This is a textbook implementation of incremental materialized view maintenance with safe degradation.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Fingerprint epoch semantics should be validated against Takeout import path
crossModuleConcerns.description: The backup pipeline (ingest/writes.rs) uses Chrome-epoch timestamps in its fingerprint regardless of browser family, while the Takeout import paths use their own source_kind values and Unix-millisecond timestamps. The doc comments state cross-flow matching is not a contract, but the Takeout import modules should be reviewed to confirm they never accidentally share a source_profile_id with the backup writer, which would cause fingerprint comparison across different epoch conventions.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Advanced search filter SQL duplication spans archive/mod.rs and archive/history.rs
crossModuleConcerns.description: The advanced search WHERE clause with 14 NOT EXISTS subqueries is duplicated across 5 SQL constants (LIST_HISTORY_SQL, COUNT_HISTORY_SQL in mod.rs, and LIST_HISTORY_LEXICAL_SQL, LIST_HISTORY_FUZZY_CANDIDATES_SQL, COUNT_HISTORY_LEXICAL_SQL in history.rs). Any future search operator addition must update all 5 locations. The FA-SEARCH focus area should verify that the tag: and note: operators recently added are consistent across all 5 SQL variants.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Intelligence rebuild progress callbacks should be verified against worker/Tauri transport
crossModuleConcerns.description: The intelligence rebuild functions accept F: FnMut(CoreIntelligenceProgress) -> Result<()> callbacks. These are consumed by the vault-worker and Tauri command layers. The worker layer should be verified to properly forward these progress events to the frontend task store without blocking or dropping events, especially during the batched visit-derive fallback path which can produce many progress updates for large archives.

focusArea: FA-INGEST: Rust Browser Parser & Takeout Pipeline
summary: Reviewed 29 files (~17,650 lines) spanning browser-history-parser (Chromium, Firefox, Safari, Takeout parsers), vault-core takeout pipeline (Browser Direct staging, import, batch review), vault-core migration (export/import bundle), and vault-core chrome (discovery, paths, staging). The codebase demonstrates strong architectural discipline: parsers are properly isolated from platform concerns, streaming batch consumption prevents unbounded memory growth for large imports, timestamp handling includes robust clamping against adversarial/corrupt sources, and cross-browser support follows a thoughtful family-based adapter pattern. Several issues are already tracked in BACKLOG (chromium/mod.rs oversized at 1559 lines, visit_duration unit mismatch, PRAGMA quick_check unbounded). The most significant new findings relate to silent data loss from INNER JOINs on orphaned visits across all three local-browser parsers, the Takeout stable_key_i64 hash function's weak collision resistance at scale, Safari URL query performance on large archives due to correlated subqueries, and an inconsistency in Firefox optional-table SELECT * queries where primary-key column assumptions may silently produce wrong entity keys.

findings.title: INNER JOIN silently drops orphaned visits across all three local-browser parsers
findings.description: The Chromium visit ingest query uses `JOIN urls ON urls.id = visits.url`, Firefox uses `JOIN moz_places ON moz_places.id = moz_historyvisits.place_id`, and Safari uses `JOIN history_items ON history_items.id = history_visits.history_item`. All three are INNER JOINs. If a browser database has orphaned visits (visit rows whose parent URL/place/item was deleted by the browser's own retention or a corrupted DB), these visits are silently dropped with no warning, no counter, and no log. For a product that positions itself as a 20-year archival trust tool, silent data loss on valid source rows is a meaningful gap.
findings.impact: Users lose orphaned visit records during import without any indication. On a real-world Chrome DB that has been through multiple retention cycles, orphaned visits can exist. The user has no way to know data was silently discarded.
findings.suggestedFix: Change to LEFT JOIN, emit visits with a null/placeholder URL when the parent row is missing, and add a parser warning when orphaned visits are detected. Alternatively, keep INNER JOIN but add a diagnostic count query comparing total visits vs joined visits and surface the difference as a ParserWarning with code 'orphaned-visits'. This is already tracked in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A as a deferred parser finding requiring Accepted-doc decision.
findings.codeSnippet:
pub const INGEST_VISITS_SQL: &str =
    "SELECT visits.id, visits.url, urls.url, urls.title, visits.visit_time, visits.from_visit,
            visits.transition, visits.visit_duration, visits.is_known_to_sync,
            visits.visited_link_id, visits.external_referrer_url, visits.app_id
     FROM visits
     JOIN urls ON urls.id = visits.url
     WHERE visits.id > ?1
     ORDER BY visits.id ASC";

findings.title: Safari URL query uses correlated subqueries that scan history_visits per URL row
findings.description: URLS_SQL contains three correlated subqueries against history_visits for each history_items row: one to get the title (with ORDER BY and LIMIT 1), one for COUNT(*) to compute visit_count, and one for MAX(visit_time) to get last_visit_time, plus another MAX(visit_time) in the WHERE clause. On a Safari History.db with many items and visits (approaching the 14.4M design ceiling), each of these correlated subqueries triggers a full scan of history_visits rows for each URL, making the query O(URLs * Visits). Unlike Chromium/Firefox where visit_count and last_visit_time are pre-materialized on the URL row, Safari's schema forces these to be computed. There is no first-import optimization path like Chromium/Firefox's URLS_FULL_SQL.
findings.impact: On a large Safari archive, the preview/import URL pass could take minutes due to the multiplicative scan cost. The 14.4M-record mental test fails for this code path.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider rewriting the Safari URL query to use a single GROUP BY join pattern that reduces four per-row scans to one grouped join. Also add a URLS_FULL_SQL path for first imports like Chromium/Firefox have.

findings.title: Takeout stable_key_i64 hash function has high collision risk at scale
findings.description: The stable_key_i64 function is used to generate both source_url_id and source_visit_id for Takeout records. It works by hex-encoding the input bytes and then folding with wrapping_mul(31).wrapping_add(byte). This is essentially a Java-style hashCode() mapped to i64 range. While the i64::MIN corner case is handled, the fundamental algorithm is a weak hash with high collision probability at scale. For 14.4M Takeout records (the design ceiling), the birthday paradox means collisions become likely well before that threshold. A collision on source_visit_id causes INSERT OR IGNORE to silently drop a legitimate visit. A collision on source_url_id causes unrelated URLs to be merged into one canonical URL row. This is already tracked as B5 in the import dedup audit and WORK-IMPORT-SCALE-TEST-A in BACKLOG.
findings.impact: At scale, legitimate visits and URLs could be silently dropped or incorrectly merged due to hash collisions. The user would see fewer records imported than exist in their Takeout export, with no error or warning.
findings.suggestedFix: Already tracked as WORK-IMPORT-SCALE-TEST-A in BACKLOG. The fix should replace the Java-style hash with a proper hash function (e.g., SipHash or FNV-1a mapped to i64), or use a cryptographic hash truncated to i64. The hex-encoding step before folding is also wasteful -- it doubles the input size before hashing.

findings.title: visit_duration_ms field name is misleading -- Chromium stores microseconds but field says milliseconds
findings.description: The ParsedVisit struct has a field visit_duration_ms: Option<i64>. In the Chromium parser (chromium/mod.rs:788), the raw visits.visit_duration column value is read directly into this field via row.get(7)?. However, Chromium's visits.visit_duration is stored in microseconds, not milliseconds. The field name visit_duration_ms implies milliseconds, but the actual value is microseconds. This is the exact issue already tracked in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A.
findings.impact: Downstream consumers that interpret the field as milliseconds (as the name says) will inflate duration values by 1000x. The E12 test has accepted/pinned this as current behavior, but the naming remains actively misleading.
findings.suggestedFix: Already tracked in BACKLOG as requiring a trade-off document and Accepted-doc decision before changing. The field should be renamed to visit_duration_source_units or the value should be converted at parser time.

findings.title: PRAGMA quick_check runs unbounded full-database scan on every Browser Direct import/preview
findings.description: The quick_check_sqlite function runs PRAGMA quick_check on the staged database. This PRAGMA scans the entire database to verify B-tree integrity. For a large Chrome History database (potentially hundreds of MB), this adds significant latency to every Browser Direct preview and import. The function is called twice in the worst case: once after staging (line 106) and once after copy fallback (line 231). Already tracked in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A.
findings.impact: For users with large browser history databases, the preview step (which should feel instant per UX principles) could take multiple seconds or longer just for integrity checking.
findings.suggestedFix: Already tracked in BACKLOG. Consider using PRAGMA quick_check(1) which limits to checking the first issue found, or removing the redundant second check after the online-backup fallback when the backup itself succeeded.

findings.title: Firefox optional-table capture assumes 'id' as primary key column for all moz_* tables
findings.description: When capturing optional Firefox tables (moz_inputhistory, moz_places_metadata, moz_places_metadata_search_queries), the code uses capture_native_rows with 'id' as the primary_key_column for all three. However, moz_inputhistory in real Firefox does not have an 'id' column -- its primary key is (place_id, input). When capture_native_row cannot find the specified primary key column in the row payload, it silently falls back to the string 'unknown' (observation.rs:131). This means all moz_inputhistory entities would share the same native_primary_key of 'unknown', making them indistinguishable in the source evidence archive.
findings.impact: Source evidence rows for moz_inputhistory may all have the same primary key, making them useless for later entity resolution or provenance tracking. The test fixture at line 604 creates moz_inputhistory WITH an id column, masking this issue in tests.
findings.suggestedFix: Use 'place_id' as the primary_key_column for moz_inputhistory, or better yet, use 'input' with parent key 'place_id'. The test fixture should match real Firefox schema (no synthetic id column).

findings.title: Firefox optional-table SELECT * is vulnerable to schema changes and uses format! for table name
findings.description: The optional Firefox table capture uses format!("SELECT * FROM {optional_table}") to construct SQL. While the table names come from a hardcoded list (so SQL injection is not a risk), using SELECT * means the parser will silently consume any new columns Firefox adds in future versions without schema observation or capability tracking. The same pattern exists in Safari (safari/mod.rs:373). The Chromium parser avoids this by using explicit column lists in all its queries.
findings.impact: Future Firefox/Safari schema additions would be captured in native entities without any schema observation update, potentially leading to silent data model drift.
findings.suggestedFix: This is acceptable for cold native-entity preservation (the purpose is to capture the full row), but should at minimum feed discovered columns into the schema observation. Consider adding the optional table names to the inspect_schema required_tables list so column shapes are observed.

findings.title: chromium/mod.rs at 1559 lines exceeds the 1400-line hard limit for new business logic
findings.description: The Chromium parser module is 1559 lines, exceeding the AGENTS.md 1400-line hard limit. Per the project rules, only bug fixes and minimal changes are allowed. This is already tracked as WORK-MAINT-PARSER-CHROMIUM-A in BACKLOG. The file mixes production parsing logic (~835 lines), test fixtures (~200 lines), and test assertions (~500 lines). The streaming functions (stream_url_batches, stream_visit_batches, stream_download_batches, stream_search_term_batches, stream_favicon_batches) each follow an identical pattern and could be extracted into a generic streaming helper.
findings.impact: Violates the AGENTS.md 1400-line hard limit. Adding new Chromium parser features or adapters requires touching this file, which is currently forbidden by project rules.
findings.suggestedFix: Already tracked as WORK-MAINT-PARSER-CHROMIUM-A in BACKLOG. The review phase should identify streaming helpers, row parsers, evidence builders, and test fixtures as separate owner modules.

findings.title: Takeout browser_history accumulator uses BTreeMap/BTreeSet for URL dedup with O(log n) per operation
findings.description: BrowserHistoryAccumulator uses BTreeMap<i64, ParsedUrl> for pending_urls and BTreeSet<i64> for seen_url_ids. For Takeout imports with millions of records, HashMap/HashSet would provide O(1) amortized operations vs O(log n) for BTree variants. The accumulated URLs are flushed in chunks, but the seen_url_ids set grows unboundedly across the entire payload.
findings.impact: Minor performance degradation on large Takeout imports. For 14.4M records, each URL dedup lookup costs ~24 comparisons instead of ~1. The total overhead is moderate but measurable.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace BTreeMap/BTreeSet with HashMap/HashSet for pending_urls and seen_url_ids. The ordering guarantee of BTree is not needed here -- URLs are flushed in chunks and the final order does not matter.

findings.title: Takeout stable_key_i64 hex-encodes before hashing, needlessly expanding input and reducing entropy
findings.description: The stable_key_i64 function first hex-encodes the input bytes, then folds over the hex string's bytes. Hex encoding doubles the input length (each byte becomes two hex chars), which wastes CPU cycles. More importantly, it reduces the effective entropy of the folding input: instead of folding over 256 possible byte values per position, it folds over only 16 (0-9, a-f). This reduces the hash quality and increases collision probability.
findings.impact: Marginal increase in collision probability and wasted CPU cycles on hex encoding. The collision risk at scale is the more serious concern (covered in a separate finding).
findings.suggestedFix: Remove the hex encoding step and fold directly over the raw bytes. This both improves hash quality (more entropy per position) and eliminates the allocation and CPU cost of hex encoding.

findings.title: Browser Direct import always starts from cursor zero -- no incremental re-import support
findings.description: stream_browser_history always passes zero-valued cursors (ChromiumReadCursor::default() for Chromium, 0/0 for Firefox and Safari). This means every Browser Direct import re-reads the entire source database from scratch. The Chromium parser supports incremental cursors (after_visit_id, after_url_last_visit_time) and the visit INSERT uses OR IGNORE to deduplicate, but the full re-read of the source database is still performed. For a large Chrome History with hundreds of thousands of entries, repeated imports waste significant time re-reading and re-hashing rows that are already in the archive.
findings.impact: Users who import the same browser profile multiple times (e.g., periodic manual imports) pay the full cost of reading every source row even when only a few new ones exist.
findings.suggestedFix: Read the last watermark from profile_watermarks for the source profile and pass it as the cursor to the parser. The watermark is already written by persist_browser_source_evidence_plan (line 782-799) but never read back for Browser Direct imports. This would make re-imports incremental like backup imports.

findings.title: Duplicated flush_source_evidence helper across Chromium, Firefox, and Safari parsers
findings.description: The flush_source_evidence function is copy-pasted across chromium/mod.rs (lines 342-367), firefox/mod.rs (lines 107-130), and safari/mod.rs (lines 127-149). All three implementations have the same signature and nearly identical logic: take the chunk, either extend the report vectors or call the consumer. Similarly, stream_sql is duplicated across all three.
findings.impact: Any behavior change to source evidence flushing must be replicated in three places. This is a maintenance burden and a potential source of inconsistency.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract flush_source_evidence and stream_sql into the shared observation module or a new parser-internal helpers module. The Safari version's merge_typed_evidence helper could serve as the canonical implementation.

findings.title: Takeout source.rs snippet_contains_browser_history_payload is fragile against Google format changes
findings.description: The content sniff for unrecognized JSON files checks for the literal strings 'Browser History' AND 'time_usec' in the first 64KB. This is fragile because: (1) Google could change the array key name in future Takeout exports, (2) the sniff reads up to 64KB which might miss the indicator in large files where the array key appears after the buffer, and (3) the check is case-sensitive while Google has been known to change casing across locales.
findings.impact: Low immediate impact since the primary path dispatch handles all known filenames. This only affects edge cases where users rename Takeout files or Google introduces new export layouts.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider making the sniff case-insensitive and adding the browser_history.rs parse_browser_record field names (url, visitTime, timeUsec, time_usec, visitedAt) as additional indicators.

findings.title: Migration bundle extraction does not validate zip entry sizes before extraction
findings.description: The extract_bundle_into function (called during apply_import) extracts zip entries based on the manifest, but the code validates paths for traversal attacks (validate_bundle_relative_path) without also checking for zip-bomb style attacks where declared sizes are small but decompressed sizes are enormous. While the manifest does include size_bytes per file, the extraction code should verify that the actual extracted size matches the manifest's declared size to prevent a malicious bundle from exhausting disk space.
findings.impact: A crafted .pathkeep-bundle could contain a zip bomb that exhausts disk space during import. The user would need to manually select and confirm the bundle, reducing but not eliminating the attack surface.
findings.suggestedFix: During extraction, compare the actual bytes written against the manifest's declared size_bytes for each file. Abort extraction if the actual size exceeds the declared size by more than a small tolerance. Also consider adding a total-size check against available disk space before starting extraction.

commendations.title: Excellent streaming batch consumer architecture prevents unbounded memory growth
commendations.description: The HistoryBatchConsumer trait and its streaming implementation across all parsers (Chromium, Firefox, Safari, Takeout) is a genuinely well-designed architecture. Instead of materializing all parsed rows in memory before archive ingest, the parser streams canonical URL/visit/download/search-term/favicon batches in bounded chunks to the consumer. The retain_source_evidence_in_report toggle lets import flows push source evidence through the consumer while preview flows retain it in the report. This directly supports the 14.4M-record design ceiling without unbounded memory growth. The chunk_size.max(1) guard at the entry of every streaming function prevents zero-sized chunks.

commendations.title: Robust timestamp clamping against corrupt/adversarial source databases
commendations.description: The timestamp handling across all parsers is impressively thorough. The clamp_unix_millis function (types.rs:28) ensures numeric and ISO representations always agree. The Safari parser specifically handles f64::INFINITY, f64::NEG_INFINITY, and f64::NAN (safari/mod.rs:412-414) with explicit is_finite() checks. The Chromium parser uses saturating_sub and div_euclid for safe arithmetic on untrusted i64 timestamps. This defensive coding prevents the exact class of bugs where a corrupt browser DB causes the archive to store nonsensical dates that break preview-range summaries and time-based queries.

commendations.title: First-import URL query optimization avoids multi-GB materialization on large archives
commendations.description: Both the Chromium and Firefox parsers implement a smart first-import optimization (INGEST_URLS_FULL_SQL / URLS_FULL_SQL) that strips the OR clause with the visits subquery when both watermarks are zero. The documentation in the code clearly explains WHY: on a 14.4M-visit history, the OR's DISTINCT subquery forces SQLite to materialize an ephemeral B-tree of every URL id, adding multi-GB transient memory and multi-minute stall. The first_import guard ensures this optimization only fires when both cursors are at zero, maintaining correctness for incremental imports. This shows the team actively thinking about the 14.4M-record mental test.

crossModuleConcerns.title: visit_duration unit mismatch propagates from parser through archive into intelligence aggregation
crossModuleConcerns.description: The Chromium parser stores visit_duration in source microseconds under a field named visit_duration_ms. The archive ingest writes this verbatim to the visits.visit_duration_ms column. The intelligence aggregation (phase_three.rs:561, outside this review scope) then interprets this as real milliseconds, inflating duration metrics by 1000x. This cross-module chain means the fix must be coordinated across parser -> archive ingest -> intelligence layers.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Browser Direct import watermarks are written but never read back for incremental re-import
crossModuleConcerns.description: The vault-core takeout/browser_history.rs module writes profile_watermarks after a successful Browser Direct import (lines 782-799), but the staging.rs module always passes zero cursors when streaming. The archive/ingest module (outside this scope) reads watermarks for backup-path incremental imports. The gap means Browser Direct imports always do full re-reads while backup imports are incremental. The ingest module's watermark-reading code should be investigated to determine if it can be reused for Browser Direct.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Migration bundle import uses fs::rename which fails across filesystem boundaries on some platforms
crossModuleConcerns.description: The migration.rs apply_import function renames staged files into the live project tree. While Codex C3 fixed the main staging directory to be under app_root/staging (same filesystem), the .bak backup rename at lines 488-497 could still fail if the project root is on a filesystem that does not support atomic rename of open WAL-mode databases. The Tauri command facade is supposed to stop workers first, but this cross-module contract is not enforced at the migration.rs level.

summary: Reviewed 20 files totaling approximately 12,258 lines across vault-platform (scheduler facade, macOS/Windows/Linux sub-modules, keyring, audit, test_support) and vault-worker (lib, archive_flows, security, cli, intelligence hierarchy, schedule, context, app, mcp, migration, job_runtime, tests). The platform integration layer is well-decomposed following the WORK-SCHED-STATE-A split; scheduler facade is 721 lines with platform owners under 900 lines each. The keyring module cleanly separates native and test backends with proper cfg gating. The worker crate correctly isolates orchestration from domain logic. Key concerns: archive_flows.rs exceeds the 1400-line hard limit at 1845 lines, worker_args slice access can panic, duplicated ensure_parent_dir, and the tests.rs file at 2461 lines concentrates too many test responsibilities.

findings.title: archive_flows.rs exceeds the 1400-line hard limit at 1845 lines
findings.description: archive_flows.rs is 1845 lines, well above the 1400-line hard limit defined in AGENTS.md. The file mixes backup orchestration, og:image worker pool management, import/takeout/export pass-throughs, doctor/repair wrappers, deterministic intelligence refresh helpers, and their corresponding test suite (~850 lines of tests). Per AGENTS.md rules, this file should only receive bug fixes or minimal changes, and a BACKLOG entry with high-priority maintainability review is required.
findings.impact: Violates the project's own file-size red line. Adding any future backup flow (e.g., readable-content fetch in v0.3) would deepen the violation. The og:image worker pool logic (refetch_og_images, drain_one_worker_url, host_throttle_wait, record_refetch_outcome) is a self-contained concern that could be its own focused module.
findings.suggestedFix: Extract the og:image worker pool and its helpers (refetch_og_images, drain_one_worker_url, host_throttle_wait, record_refetch_outcome, lock_or_recover, finalize_refetch_run, try_refetch_due_og_images, try_prefetch_new_visit_og_images, prefetch_og_images_on_demand, effective_og_image_fetch_mode, and all append_og_image_* helpers) into a focused archive_flows/og_image_worker.rs module. Move the corresponding tests alongside. This should bring archive_flows.rs well under 1000 lines. Add a BACKLOG entry for this work.

findings.title: worker_args[1..] slice can panic if worker_args is empty
findings.description: Both windows.rs (line 93) and linux.rs (line 36) index worker_args[1..] without checking that worker_args has at least one element. If worker_args is empty, this will panic at runtime with an out-of-bounds slice index. The caller in scheduler.rs currently always passes a non-empty vec (lines 46-51), but the function signatures accept &[String] with no documented precondition, making this fragile to future callers.
findings.impact: Any future refactoring that changes how worker_args is constructed (e.g., adding a new schedule command variant that passes an empty args vec) would cause a panic in production. This violates the defensive programming principle for a platform integration layer.
findings.suggestedFix: Either add a guard at the top of both functions: `let tail_args = if worker_args.len() > 1 { &worker_args[1..] } else { &[] };` or document the precondition explicitly with a debug_assert!(worker_args.len() >= 1). The macos.rs module already handles this correctly in macos_program_arguments (lines 205-209) by checking whether worker_args is non-empty before slicing.
findings.codeSnippet: xml_escape(&worker_args[1..].join(" "))

findings.title: Duplicated ensure_parent_dir function in scheduler facade and audit module
findings.description: ensure_parent_dir is defined identically in two places: scheduler.rs (line 146) with pub(super) visibility (used by windows.rs) and audit.rs (line 136) as a private function. Both have the same implementation: check for parent directory and create_dir_all.
findings.impact: Minor code duplication that could lead to drift. Both are small (5 lines), but the duplication is unnecessary since the audit module is a child of the scheduler module and could use the parent's version.
findings.suggestedFix: Remove the private copy in audit.rs and import the pub(super) version from the parent scheduler module via `use super::ensure_parent_dir;`.

findings.title: tests.rs at 2461 lines concentrates too many test responsibilities
findings.description: The worker test file is 2461 lines and covers app snapshot flows, CLI routing, MCP surface, AI queue orchestration, provider resolution, security/lock status, coverage-specific drains, and runtime execution paths. Per AGENTS.md, files above 1200 lines should have a maintainability review in BACKLOG, and above 1400 lines should only receive bug fixes.
findings.impact: The file is difficult to navigate and adding new worker tests will only deepen the problem. Test isolation is harder when all coverage-sensitive paths are concentrated in one module.
findings.suggestedFix: Split into focused test modules: tests/cli_tests.rs, tests/mcp_tests.rs, tests/ai_queue_tests.rs, tests/security_tests.rs, and tests/archive_flow_tests.rs, with a shared test fixtures module for chrome_user_data_fixture, takeout_fixture, and env helpers. Add a BACKLOG entry.

findings.title: og:image refetch loads config from disk three times in the post-backup path
findings.description: The run_backup_now_with_progress function loads config at the top (line 104), then again at line 177 for budget lookups, and the comment at lines 174-176 explicitly acknowledges that try_refetch_due_og_images and try_prefetch_new_visit_og_images will each load config internally. The code comments note this is 'non-trivial on a hot post-backup path' but the dedup was only applied to the budget reads, not the actual refetch/prefetch helpers.
findings.impact: On the post-backup hot path, load_unlocked_config is called 5 times (backup, budget, refetch, prefetch, cleanup). Each call parses the on-disk JSON. With 14.4M records and frequent backup ticks, this adds measurable I/O overhead. The code comments indicate awareness but the fix was partial.
findings.suggestedFix: Thread the already-loaded config and paths from the top of run_backup_now_with_progress into the refetch/prefetch/cleanup helpers as parameters instead of having each one re-read config from disk. This would require adjusting the function signatures of try_refetch_due_og_images, try_prefetch_new_visit_og_images, and run_og_image_cleanup to accept config + paths.

findings.title: Keyring test backend redirect is gated by debug_assertions, not a compile-time feature
findings.description: The test_keyring_dir() function for non-coverage builds gates the env-var redirect with cfg!(debug_assertions). The comment at lines 85-89 explains the reasoning well: release builds must never honor the redirect. However, debug_assertions can be enabled in custom release profiles (e.g., release-with-debug), and cfg!(debug_assertions) is a runtime check not a compile-time gate, meaning the code for reading the env var is always compiled into the binary.
findings.impact: A custom cargo profile that enables debug_assertions in release mode would make the env-var redirect active in a shipped binary. The risk is low because Tauri's default release profile correctly disables debug_assertions, and the comment shows clear security awareness.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider using a dedicated cargo feature (e.g., `test-keyring-redirect`) instead of relying on debug_assertions, or add a compile-time assertion in the build.rs that verifies debug_assertions is off when building with the release profile. The current approach is reasonable but could be made more robust.

findings.title: Windows schedule XML uses hardcoded 2026 StartBoundary date
findings.description: The Windows Task Scheduler XML template hardcodes StartBoundary to 2026-01-01T09:00:00 for the TimeTrigger. While this works because the Repetition interval is what actually drives periodic execution, Windows Task Scheduler treats StartBoundary as a filter: the task will not fire until this date has passed.
findings.impact: If a user installs PathKeep before 2026 (current year is 2026, so this is marginal) or if the date is changed to a future year during maintenance, the timer trigger would not fire until that date. The hardcoded date also means the first trigger after install waits for 2026-01-01T09:00 to pass before the repetition kicks in (though StartWhenAvailable=true mitigates missed triggers).
findings.suggestedFix: Consider using the current date/time as the StartBoundary (e.g., Utc::now() formatted as ISO 8601) so the timer begins immediately after registration. This would also make the generated XML differ per-install, which is a trade-off for deterministic testing.

findings.title: Linux schedule plan does not implement status detection or repair
findings.description: The Linux schedule module generates systemd unit files but has no status detection, apply, remove, or repair functionality. The scheduler facade returns 'manual-review' for Linux install_state with a warning about only macOS having detection. This asymmetry is documented but means Linux users get no feedback about whether their timer is actually installed and running.
findings.impact: Linux users who manually install the systemd timer have no way to verify the installation state through PathKeep's UI. The schedule page will always show 'manual-review' even after successful installation.
findings.suggestedFix: Add basic status detection via systemctl --user is-active and systemctl --user cat to at least report installed/not-installed state on Linux. This would bring Linux closer to parity with macOS and Windows. Consider tracking this as a v0.3 or post-release enhancement.

findings.title: Raw English strings in scheduler warning messages instead of i18n keys
findings.description: The scheduler facade and its sub-modules mix i18n-ready keys (e.g., schedule.verifyMacosBootstrapOk) with raw English warning strings (e.g., 'Automatic install-status detection is only implemented on macOS in v1' at line 126, 'Apply is only implemented on macOS and Windows in v1' at line 139). The manual_steps arrays also contain raw English strings. AGENTS.md requires all user-visible copy to be delivered in en/zh-CN/zh-TW.
findings.impact: Users running PathKeep with Chinese locale will see English-only messages for platform-specific warnings and unsupported-action messages. The i18n keys exist for structured data (issues, verification checks) but the unstructured warnings and messages are raw English.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace raw English strings in the message field of ApplyResult and the warnings push calls with i18n keys that the frontend can resolve. The manual_steps and manual_step_details already use key-based titles/summaries, so extending the pattern to the remaining strings would be consistent.

findings.title: refetch_og_images creates a new reqwest client per call instead of reusing across ticks
findings.description: Every call to refetch_og_images (which can happen multiple times per backup tick: refetch-due, prefetch-new-visits, on-demand) builds a new reqwest HTTP client via build_fetch_client(). The client constructor performs TLS root certificate loading and runtime initialization, which test comments note can take ~150ms on CI (line 1251).
findings.impact: On the post-backup hot path where both refetch and prefetch run sequentially, the TLS/root-cert initialization happens twice. With daily backup ticks this is negligible, but with minute-level intervals it adds measurable latency.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider caching the FetchClient in a thread-local or passing it from the caller. Since the client is Send + Sync (it is already Arc-wrapped for the worker pool), a single build at the start of the post-backup sequence could serve all three og:image passes.

commendations.title: Excellent scheduler decomposition after the WORK-SCHED-STATE-A split
commendations.description: The scheduler module was correctly identified as exceeding the 1400-line limit and was decomposed into a 721-line facade with focused platform owners (macos.rs, windows.rs, linux.rs, audit.rs). Each sub-module has clear doc headers with Responsibilities / Not responsible for / Dependencies / Performance notes sections, making the boundary intent unambiguous. The split preserved all existing test coverage and the facade cleanly delegates to platform-specific implementations.

commendations.title: Principled keyring test isolation with security-aware release gating
commendations.description: The keyring module's dual-backend design (native keyring for production, file-backed for tests/coverage) is well-executed. The test_support.rs module's explicit comment at lines 85-89 explaining why release builds must never honor the env-var redirect shows genuine security thinking. The coverage-mode backend uses OnceLock for thread-safe initialization, and the file-backed helpers use context-annotated errors throughout.

commendations.title: macOS launch constraint workaround is thoroughly documented and tested
commendations.description: The macos_program_arguments function (lines 195-234) addresses a real macOS 14+ launch constraint issue with exceptional documentation: the doc comment explains the SIGKILL cause, why /usr/bin/open -n -W -g -a bypasses it, what each flag does, and what the long-term architecture should be (dedicated worker sidecar). Four targeted tests cover bundled, nested, unbundled, and no-leading-exe-copy edge cases. This is a model for how platform-specific workarounds should be documented.

commendations.title: Worker og:image pool demonstrates robust error isolation
commendations.description: The og:image refetch worker pool in archive_flows.rs shows careful error isolation: lock_or_recover prevents mutex poisoning from one panicking worker from taking down the rest; per-host throttling prevents rate-limit abuse; the mpsc channel pattern keeps SQLite writes single-threaded while network I/O is parallel; and record_refetch_outcome captures the last persist error without losing the success count. The design comment at line 500 about reverse+pop for FIFO without O(n) drain costs shows performance awareness.

commendations.title: Security mutation gating prevents lock bypass through IPC
commendations.description: The security.rs module correctly routes configure_app_lock_passcode and remove_app_lock_passcode through load_unlocked_config, which means these mutations are refused when the app session is locked. The doc comments at lines 237-241 and 253-256 explicitly explain the security reasoning: without this gate, an actor with IPC access could overwrite the passcode while the app is locked. The test at lines 555-570 in tests.rs verifies all three mutation gates (configure, remove, save_config).

crossModuleConcerns.title: archive_flows.rs 1845-line file needs BACKLOG entry for maintainability split
crossModuleConcerns.description: archive_flows.rs is at 1845 lines, exceeding the 1400-line hard limit. The og:image worker pool (~350 lines production + ~300 lines tests), the deterministic intelligence refresh helpers, and the import/takeout pass-throughs are distinct concerns that could be extracted. The BACKLOG currently tracks scheduler and parser splits but not this file.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Raw English strings in scheduler and security warning messages need i18n audit
crossModuleConcerns.description: The scheduler facade, its platform sub-modules, and the security module contain raw English strings in user-visible warning messages, manual_steps text, and ApplyResult message fields. The i18n contract requires en/zh-CN/zh-TW delivery. A cross-cutting sweep of all backend-generated user-visible strings should be tracked.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: FA-I18N review area, schedule page frontend, security page frontend

crossModuleConcerns.title: tests.rs at 2461 lines should be tracked alongside other test suite maintainability items
crossModuleConcerns.description: vault-worker/src/tests.rs is 2461 lines, joining archive/tests.rs (3272 lines, already tracked as WORK-ARCHIVE-TEST-MAINT-A) as an oversized test concentration. Both follow the same pattern of accumulating coverage-driven tests in a single file.

focusArea: FA-BRIDGE: Tauri IPC Bridge & Desktop Contract
summary: Reviewed the entire Tauri IPC trust boundary: src-tauri/src/ (lib.rs, main.rs, session.rs, commands/*.rs, worker_bridge/*.rs, dev_ipc_bridge/, updater.rs, file_manager.rs, test_support.rs), src/lib/ipc/ (bridge.ts and progress listeners), src/lib/backend-client/ (all 15 files), and src/lib/types/ (all 8 files). The bridge is architecturally sound -- the command facade is consistently thin, the worker_bridge layer properly isolates session state management, the off-main-thread pattern is applied to long-running operations, and the frontend backend-client provides a typed surface that hides raw command names. Test coverage is extensive with comprehensive integration tests. The main findings relate to synchronous SQLite-touching commands that could block the WebView main thread under 14.4M-record loads, legacy naming artifacts from the pre-PathKeep era, and a file size threshold approaching the 1200-line review marker in worker_bridge/mod.rs.

findings.title: 36 synchronous Core Intelligence read commands run on the Tauri main thread
