findings.description: All 36 Core Intelligence read-model commands in commands/intelligence/core.rs (get_sessions, get_search_trails, get_hub_pages, get_top_sites, get_domain_trend, get_refind_pages, get_activity_mix, get_digest_summary, get_browsing_rhythm, get_discovery_trend, etc.) are defined as synchronous `fn` rather than `async fn` backed by `run_blocking_command`. These commands query SQLite through vault-worker and vault-core, which means they execute on the Tauri main (UI) thread. Under the 14.4M-record design ceiling, any of these queries could take hundreds of milliseconds, freezing the WebView during the read. The runtime.rs sibling correctly uses `async fn` + `run_blocking_command` for the three overview and runtime commands, proving the pattern is known. The doc header even states: 'Commands in this file must stay as thin read-model facades ... this layer must not add full-archive materialization.' -- but the enforcement is missing because thin does not mean fast on a 14M-row archive.
findings.impact: On archives approaching the 14.4M design ceiling, navigating Intelligence routes will cause intermittent WebView freezes (spinner stops, repaint hangs) because the main thread is blocked in SQLite I/O. This directly contradicts the 'UI must not freeze' constraint in AGENTS.md.
findings.suggestedFix: Convert all 36 synchronous commands to `async fn` + `run_blocking_command`, mirroring the pattern already used in runtime.rs for get_intelligence_primary_overview and load_intelligence_runtime. The worker_bridge functions can stay synchronous -- only the Tauri command facade needs the async wrapper.

findings.title: Several archive commands remain synchronous despite touching SQLite
findings.description: load_dashboard_snapshot (line 235), get_browse_day_insights (line 247), load_audit_run_detail (line 257), export_history (line 267), doctor_report (line 277), repair_health (line 286), and clear_derived_intelligence (line 295) are all synchronous `fn` commands that read/write the archive SQLite database on the Tauri main thread. The same file correctly uses `async fn` + `run_blocking_command` for query_history, load_history_favicons, og:image operations, and run_backup_now. The inconsistency suggests these were written before the off-main-thread pattern was established and never migrated.
findings.impact: Dashboard load, audit detail, export, and doctor operations could freeze the WebView on large archives. Dashboard and Browse day-insights are hit on every route navigation, making this user-visible.
findings.suggestedFix: Convert these seven commands to `async fn` backed by `run_blocking_command`, consistent with the pattern already in use for query_history and og:image commands in the same file.

findings.title: rekey_archive runs synchronously despite being a heavy archive mutation
findings.description: rekey_archive is a synchronous `fn` that performs archive re-encryption (or mode switch), which can involve reading and rewriting the entire archive database. For a 14.4M-record archive, this is a multi-second to multi-minute operation. initialize_archive correctly uses async + run_blocking_command, but rekey does not, even though the desktop-command-surface.md notes from 2026-04-20 explicitly state: 'archive init / backup / takeout scan/import ... must go async + spawn_blocking, avoid WebView main-thread freeze. Future long-running commands follow the same off-main-thread contract.'
findings.impact: Rekey on a large encrypted archive will freeze the WebView for the entire duration of the re-encryption, potentially minutes. The user cannot see progress or cancel.
findings.suggestedFix: Convert rekey_archive (and likely preview_rekey_archive, preview_snapshot_restore, run_snapshot_restore, preview_retention_prune, run_retention_prune) to async + run_blocking_command.

findings.title: worker_bridge/mod.rs at 1220 lines -- approaching 1200-line review threshold
findings.description: worker_bridge/mod.rs is 1220 lines, just above the AGENTS.md 1200-line review threshold. The file contains the worker_result helper, all test imports, and five large integration test functions (command_helpers_cover_local_desktop_flows at ~500 lines, worker_bridge_covers_dashboard_audit_and_ai_wrapper_edges at ~150 lines, annotations_and_og_image_impls_cover_local_desktop_flows at ~180 lines, app_lock_bridge_guards at ~70 lines, migration_and_browse_insights at ~80 lines). The actual production code in this file is only ~70 lines; the rest is test infrastructure.
findings.impact: Per AGENTS.md, files over 1200 lines should have a maintainability analysis task added to BACKLOG. The tests are valuable but make the file hard to navigate. This is a review threshold, not a hard limit.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a BACKLOG entry for worker_bridge/mod.rs maintainability review. The most natural split would be extracting the integration tests into a sibling tests.rs module (matching the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase per module-boundary-map.md's 2026-04-14 note about test extraction).

findings.title: Legacy CHB_ and BHB_ environment variable prefixes retained in test support and build info
findings.description: test_support.rs uses CHB_ prefixed env vars (CHB_PROJECT_ROOT, CHB_CHROME_USER_DATA_DIR, CHB_TEST_KEYRING_DIR) where CHB stands for 'Chrome History Backup', the pre-rename product name. worker_bridge/app.rs uses BHB_ prefixed compile-time env vars (BHB_GIT_COMMIT_SHORT, BHB_GIT_COMMIT_FULL, BHB_GIT_DIRTY). The naming migration baseline in repo-baseline.md acknowledges that 'remaining old-name strings are controlled legacy references.' However, these test-only env vars are not controlled references -- they are just un-renamed constants.
findings.impact: No functional impact. Purely a naming consistency issue. The repo-baseline.md already classifies this as accepted legacy.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider renaming to PATHKEEP_ prefix in a future naming cleanup pass. This is low priority since they only affect test fixtures and build scripts, not production user-facing strings.

findings.title: record_frontend_error bypasses worker_bridge pattern by calling vault_core directly
findings.description: record_frontend_error is the only Tauri command in the entire facade that calls vault_core directly (vault_core::project_paths() and vault_core::record_frontend_error()) instead of going through the worker_bridge layer. Every other command follows the commands -> worker_bridge -> vault_worker -> vault_core chain. This breaks the architectural invariant stated in module-boundary-map.md that src-tauri/src/ is 'only an IPC facade'.
findings.impact: Minor architectural inconsistency. The function is simple (project_paths + write a crash report), so the practical risk is low. But it sets a precedent for future commands to bypass the bridge.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the implementation to a worker_bridge::support module (or add a vault_worker entrypoint) and have the command delegate through worker_result, consistent with every other command in the surface.

findings.title: clear_derived_intelligence command lives in archive.rs instead of intelligence module
findings.description: clear_derived_intelligence is defined in commands/archive.rs, but it operates on rebuildable intelligence state, not canonical archive data. The desktop-command-surface.md maps it to the intelligence domain. The sibling intelligence/ module (with its core.rs, ai.rs, runtime.rs split) is the natural home for this command. The worker_bridge side (clear_derived_intelligence_impl) also lives in archive.rs rather than in the intelligence subtree.
findings.impact: Minor organizational inconsistency. A developer looking for intelligence-related commands would not find this one in the intelligence module.
findings.suggestedFix: Move clear_derived_intelligence to commands/intelligence/runtime.rs and the _impl to worker_bridge/intelligence/runtime.rs in a future cleanup pass.

findings.title: Frontend BrowseDayInsights type defined locally in explorer.ts instead of shared types/
findings.description: BrowseDayInsights and its sub-types (BrowseDayTopDomain, BrowseDayTopUrl, BrowseDaySearchQuery) are defined inline in backend-client/explorer.ts rather than in src/lib/types/archive.ts where all other backend response types live. This means consumers outside the explorer scope cannot import the type from the canonical types barrel without also pulling in the explorer client. The pattern in every other backend-client file is to import types from '../types' and only define the client object locally.
findings.impact: Limits reusability of the BrowseDayInsights type. Dashboard or Intelligence surfaces that might want to render browse-day data would need to import from the explorer client module instead of the types barrel.
findings.suggestedFix: Move BrowseDayInsights, BrowseDayTopDomain, BrowseDayTopUrl, and BrowseDaySearchQuery to src/lib/types/archive.ts and re-export from the types index, then import them in explorer.ts.

findings.title: UrlAnnotation types defined locally in annotations.ts instead of shared types/
findings.description: UrlAnnotation, SetNotesRequest, and ReplaceTagsRequest are defined inline in backend-client/annotations.ts rather than in the shared types/ barrel. Every other backend-client module (app, archive, import, intelligence, schedule, security) imports its types from '../types'. The annotations module defines them locally, breaking the pattern.
findings.impact: Same as the BrowseDayInsights finding -- limits reusability. Any component that needs the UrlAnnotation shape must import from the annotations client module.
findings.suggestedFix: Move these types to a new src/lib/types/annotations.ts or add them to src/lib/types/archive.ts, then re-export from the types index.

findings.title: Migration types (ExportedBundle, ImportPreview, ImportResult, ApplyImportOptions) defined locally in migration.ts
findings.description: The migration client defines ExportedBundle, ExportManifest, ExportManifestFile, ImportExclusionNote, ImportPreview, ApplyImportOptions, ImportResult, and two error prefix constants locally. These are the only typed backend response shapes not in src/lib/types/. The pattern break is the same as the annotations and browse-day-insights findings.
findings.impact: Limited reusability outside the migration client. Settings route imports these from the migration module directly rather than from the shared types barrel.
findings.suggestedFix: Move these types to src/lib/types/ and re-export from the index, keeping the migration.ts client file focused on the call wrappers only.

commendations.title: Exemplary thin-facade architecture with complete 1:1 command-to-bridge mapping
commendations.description: Every Tauri command across all 12 command submodules (130+ commands) follows the exact same pattern: validate transport-level types, pull session state, delegate to a named worker_bridge _impl function. The facade never contains business logic, never opens databases, never makes decisions about archive semantics. This is exactly the boundary the module-boundary-map.md prescribes, and the consistency across 130+ commands is remarkable.

commendations.title: Comprehensive integration test suite validates the full Tauri-to-worker pipeline
commendations.description: worker_bridge/mod.rs contains five thorough integration tests that exercise the complete desktop pipeline: archive init, backup, query, takeout import, batch revert/restore, rekey, schedule preview/apply/remove, doctor, security, app lock, annotations, og:image, migration export/import, browse day insights, and Core Intelligence rebuilds. The tests use real SQLite fixtures with Chrome schema-faithful tables, validating that serde types round-trip correctly between Rust and the archive. This is the kind of vertical integration testing that catches type drift early.

commendations.title: Clean typed frontend client surface with consistent domain decomposition
commendations.description: The src/lib/backend-client/ layer provides a well-organized typed facade that completely hides raw Tauri command strings from page components. Each domain (app, archive, explorer, import, intelligence, schedule, security, migration, audit, dashboard, support, update, annotations) has its own focused client file, and the index.ts barrel re-exports a unified 'backend' object for callers that prefer the legacy single-import pattern. The shared.ts call() function adds production-grade command metrics (duration, request/response byte estimation) with bounded buffer retention, making IPC performance visible during development without any runtime overhead in production builds.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Core Intelligence route components should verify off-main-thread enforcement
crossModuleConcerns.description: The FA-BRIDGE review found that all 36 Core Intelligence read-model commands in commands/intelligence/core.rs are synchronous, meaning they block the Tauri main thread during SQLite queries. The FA-INTEL review area (if it exists) should verify whether the frontend Intelligence route components show loading/skeleton states during these calls, since if the command blocks the main thread, the loading state may never paint at all.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Frontend og:image fetch-mode enforcement relies on bridge-layer short-circuit
crossModuleConcerns.description: The data-sovereignty promise that 'Off = no fetching anywhere' for og:image is enforced in worker_bridge/archive.rs::refetch_og_images_impl via a match on effective_og_image_fetch_mode(). The frontend useExplorerOgImages hook calls triggerOgImageRefetch without checking the fetch mode, relying entirely on the backend to refuse. If any frontend path calls the Tauri command directly (bypassing the backend-client), the promise holds only because the bridge enforces it. Other review areas covering Explorer or Settings should verify there is no alternative fetch path.

crossModuleConcerns.title: App Lock refusal path coverage for new read-model commands
crossModuleConcerns.description: The desktop-command-surface.md requires that archive data read commands refuse in locked state. The existing test (app_lock_bridge_guards_desktop_read_models_until_unlock) only checks app_snapshot and dashboard_snapshot. The 36 Core Intelligence read commands, Browse day insights, audit detail, og:image stats, and annotation reads are not tested under the locked-session scenario. The locking behavior likely works because vault_worker internally checks session state, but the bridge-level guarantee is not verified for these commands.

focusArea: XA-PRODUCT: Product Completeness Review (src/pages/, src/components/, src/app/ routes, full user journey analysis)
summary: Reviewed 280 source files across src/pages/ (158 non-test files, ~39,901 lines), src/components/ (105 files), and src/app/ (17 files). PathKeep delivers a remarkably complete local-first browsing history archive system with three functional domains (Archive, Recall, Intelligence) working in concert. The router declares 14 named routes plus an onboarding utility route; every route documented in screens-and-nav.md exists and is wired with lazy loading and error boundaries. The first-time user journey (onboarding -> first backup -> browse -> search -> intelligence) is fully navigable. v0.3-deferred features (AI Assistant, semantic/hybrid search) are honestly communicated via i18n-backed "Coming in v0.3" copy. The most significant product gap is the schedule page at 1,308 lines -- well above the 1,200-line review threshold and approaching the 1,400-line hard limit -- which is already tracked in BACKLOG as WORK-SCHEDULE-PAGE-MAINT-A. Overall, the product is in strong shipping shape for v0.2 with solid trust, transparency, and recoverability surfaces.

findings.title: Dashboard "Active Threads" card links all navigate to /intelligence overview rather than specific thread
findings.description: Both the `onOpenAll` and `onOpenThread` callbacks on DashboardActiveThreads navigate to the same `/intelligence` URL. The `onOpenThread` callback should take users to the specific path-flow or thread entity, but instead it drops all context and sends them to the Intelligence overview page. This means clicking on a specific thread card is indistinguishable from clicking 'See all' -- the user loses the context they wanted to drill into.
findings.impact: Users who see an interesting recurring browsing pattern on the Dashboard and click to learn more are dumped at the generic Intelligence overview instead of seeing the specific thread they clicked. This breaks the Dashboard-to-Intelligence drill-down journey and creates a confusing dead-end where the user must manually locate the thread they just saw.
findings.suggestedFix: Pass the path-flow ID through `onOpenThread` and navigate to the specific shared insights route, e.g. `/intelligence/trail/:trailId` or the appropriate entity route. The DashboardActiveThreads component already renders individual thread cards with flow data; the href just needs to carry the entity identity.
findings.codeSnippet:
onOpenAll={() => void navigate('/intelligence')}
onOpenThread={() => void navigate('/intelligence')}

findings.title: Schedule page at 1,308 lines exceeds 1,200-line review threshold
findings.description: The schedule route file is a 1,308-line monolith that renders all five schedule states (CHECKING, NOT_INSTALLED, INSTALLED_OK, INSTALLED_WARN, INSTALLED_ERROR) plus interval selection, profile summaries, manual installation steps, and verification flows inline. Per AGENTS.md, files above 1,200 lines trigger a mandatory maintainability review. This is already tracked as WORK-SCHEDULE-PAGE-MAINT-A in BACKLOG (blocked on a dedicated window), but the file is only 92 lines away from the 1,400-line hard limit where no new business logic is allowed.
findings.impact: Any future change to the schedule page risks pushing it past the hard limit. The file's complexity also makes it harder to reason about individual schedule states, increasing the risk of regressions when adding features or fixing bugs in the scheduling workflow.
findings.suggestedFix: Prioritize WORK-SCHEDULE-PAGE-MAINT-A: extract each state panel (NotInstalledState, InstalledOkState, InstalledWarnState, etc.) into its own focused component file, leaving the route shell as a thin state-machine dispatcher. This is already the planned approach in the BACKLOG entry.

findings.title: Dashboard year heatmap onSelectDate uses on-this-day source parameter instead of date browsing
findings.description: When a user clicks a day cell in the Dashboard year heatmap, the navigation goes to `/explorer?date=...&source=on-this-day`. The `source=on-this-day` parameter is semantically incorrect here -- the user is browsing the heatmap, not the 'On This Day' card. This parameter may cause misleading analytics or behavior downstream if the Explorer ever differentiates based on `source`.
findings.impact: Low immediate impact but introduces a subtle semantic inconsistency. If any future feature key on the `source` parameter (e.g., for context-aware Explorer behavior), heatmap clicks would be misclassified as 'On This Day' interactions rather than heatmap drill-downs.
findings.suggestedFix: Use `source=year-heatmap` or `source=dashboard-heatmap` to accurately reflect the entry point, or remove the source parameter if it is not consumed downstream.

findings.title: Integrations page empty state action links to /security instead of /onboarding
findings.description: When the Integrations page cannot load the snapshot (archive not initialized), the empty state shows a 'Review security' action linking to `/security`. But if the archive is not initialized, the user should be directed to `/onboarding` to set up their archive first, not to the security page. The security page itself shows 'Not set up yet' without meaningful content when uninitialized.
findings.impact: A user who discovers Integrations before completing onboarding will be sent on a confusing detour through Security rather than being guided to the setup flow. This creates a broken cross-page journey where the error recovery action does not actually resolve the underlying issue.
findings.suggestedFix: Change the empty state action to link to `/onboarding` with appropriate copy like 'Complete setup first', matching the pattern used by Dashboard, Explorer, Audit, and Jobs empty states.

findings.title: Lock page contains hardcoded 'PathKeep' brand text not wrapped in i18n
findings.description: The lock page renders `PathKeep` as a hardcoded string. While the product name might intentionally remain untranslated, the i18n contract in UX principles states all user-visible copy should go through the translation system. Other brand references (e.g., in onboarding shell) also use hardcoded 'PATHKEEP' text. If this is intentional (brand names staying in Latin), it should at least be consistent -- the lock page uses 'PathKeep' (mixed case) while the onboarding header uses 'PATHKEEP' (uppercase).
findings.impact: Minor inconsistency in brand presentation between the lock screen and onboarding. Does not affect functionality but undermines the paper-archival editorial polish the design aims for.
findings.suggestedFix: Either extract the brand name into a shared constant (e.g., `BRAND_NAME = 'PathKeep'`) ensuring consistent casing everywhere, or document in AGENTS.md that the brand name is an explicit i18n exception.

findings.title: Error boundary recovery offers /jobs link but not route-specific repair actions
findings.description: The ShellRouteErrorBoundary provides three recovery actions: Retry (page reload), Open Jobs, and Go to Dashboard. This is a reasonable default, but per the UX trust warning grammar, error states should 'directly attach next-step or repair entry'. The current boundary has no way to know which route crashed, so it cannot offer route-specific repair (e.g., 'Go to Security' if the Explorer crashed due to a locked archive). Adding error context propagation from the route handle could enable smarter recovery suggestions.
findings.impact: When a route crashes, users get generic recovery options rather than targeted repair guidance. The difference between a crash from a locked archive vs. a rendering bug vs. a backend timeout all present the same three buttons.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider reading the route handle from `useMatches()` to determine which route crashed and offering route-specific secondary actions (e.g., for Explorer crashes: 'Check archive status' linking to /security; for Intelligence crashes: 'Check background jobs' linking to /jobs).

findings.title: Assistant deferred state still renders conversation panel infrastructure when AI is disabled
findings.description: The AssistantPage initializes full conversation state (messages array, input state, sending flag, provider probe state, queue action state) even when the page will render the 'Coming in v0.3' deferred state. Per screens-and-nav.md, the v0.2 Assistant 'does not display seeded prompts, settings fix entry, or queue operations' in disabled state. While the deferred rendering correctly shows only honesty copy, the hook setup runs unconditionally. This is not a bug, but the component could short-circuit earlier to avoid initializing unused state.
findings.impact: Minimal performance impact (a few unused useState calls), but makes the code slightly harder to reason about -- readers must trace past all the state initialization to find where the deferred branch actually renders.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the deferred/disabled check above the state initialization so the component can return early with the PaperAssistantView deferred surface before allocating conversation infrastructure. Guard with a comment explaining why early return is safe.

findings.title: Maintenance page reuses Settings loading copy key instead of its own
findings.description: The Maintenance route's loading state displays `t('settings.loadingModules')` -- a key from the Settings namespace. This means the loading label says something about Settings modules when the user is actually on the Maintenance page. The same pattern appears in the Integrations page loading state.
findings.impact: If a user sees the loading state, the label references 'Settings' modules which could be momentarily confusing since they navigated to Maintenance. This is a minor copy accuracy issue.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a dedicated `maintenance.loadingModules` i18n key (in all three locales) and use it here, or use a generic `common.loadingContent` key that is route-agnostic.

findings.title: No dedicated /explorer export path visible from product surfaces
findings.description: The screens-and-nav.md specification lists 'export' as a core capability of History Explorer. While the v0.2 legacy UI had an export button (retired in WORK-V03-LEGACY-RETIRE), the current paper Explorer browse and search surfaces do not appear to surface an export CTA from the reviewed code. The paper-contact-sheet and paper-search-view modules do not show export controls. The data-migration export in Settings is for whole-archive migration, not selective history export. This means users cannot export filtered search results or specific date ranges to share or back up independently.
findings.impact: Users who want to export a subset of their history (e.g., 'all visits to github.com last month' or 'search results for a specific query') have no path to do so from the current paper UI. This was explicitly a capability in the v0.2 legacy UI that was retired during the paper redesign.
findings.suggestedFix: Add an export action to the paper Explorer view (both Browse and Search surfaces) that exports the current visible/filtered results to JSONL or CSV. The backend export command likely still exists; the gap is the UI entry point.

findings.title: Dashboard 'This Week' card shows aggregate counts without actionable drill-down
findings.description: The DashboardThisWeek component receives `totalPages`, `totalUrls`, and `recentRunsCount` but from the reviewed props it only displays aggregate numbers. Per the screens-and-nav.md specification, Dashboard should provide 'quick action entry points' to Explorer, Intelligence, and Settings. The This Week card appears to be informational only without deep-link CTAs that take the user to the Explorer filtered to this week's activity.
findings.impact: The This Week summary shows users 'what happened' but does not help them act on that information. A user who sees high activity this week has no one-click path to explore it in detail.
findings.suggestedFix: Add a 'See this week in Explorer' CTA to the This Week card that navigates to `/explorer?start=<monday>&end=<today>`, following the same pattern as On This Day's `onJumpToDate` and the year heatmap's `onSelectDate`.

commendations.title: Comprehensive and consistent empty/error/loading state coverage
commendations.description: Every route in the router configuration handles loading, error, and uninitialized states with dedicated fallback components. The Dashboard route fallback state machine (route-fallback-state.ts) is particularly well-designed -- it uses a pure synchronous resolver to deterministically map shell bootstrap inputs to exactly one of five fallback branches (loading, onboarding-zero-state, unlock-required, read-error, archive-unavailable). This separation of state resolution from rendering is a pattern that keeps the main Dashboard composition clean and makes the fallback logic independently testable. The consistent use of shared primitives (EmptyState, ErrorState, LoadingState, SkeletonExplorer, DashboardSkeleton) across all routes creates a predictable user experience regardless of which page encounters an issue.

commendations.title: Honest and well-structured v0.3 deferred feature communication
commendations.description: The handling of v0.3-deferred features (AI Assistant, semantic search, MCP integrations) is exemplary. The Assistant page renders clear 'Coming in v0.3' honesty copy in all three locales without showing misleading controls (no seeded prompts, no provider test buttons, no queue operations). The Explorer's semantic/hybrid mode controls are disabled with explicit deferred messaging. The i18n catalog (assistant.ts) ships complete deferred copy across en/zh-CN/zh-TW with nuanced language ('The path is still extending...') that treats future features as aspirational rather than broken. This directly fulfills the product's Intelligence Is Optional principle -- the app is complete without AI, and it is honest about what is not yet available.

commendations.title: App Lock routing boundary enforces true security separation
commendations.description: The route-guards.tsx implementation creates a clean security boundary where the lock screen (RequireLockScreen) and the unlocked shell (RequireUnlockedShell) are mutually exclusive states, with proper redirect handling including `?next=` parameter preservation so users return to their intended destination after unlocking. The LockPage itself is designed with the paper aesthetic (serif title, mono kicker, PaperCard form) while maintaining minimal chrome -- no sidebar, no navigation, just the unlock form. The implementation correctly handles the edge case where appLockStatus is null during initial load (shows a loading gate) and when lock is disabled (redirects to next path). The onboarding shell also properly gates behind the unlock check, ensuring the security boundary is enforced consistently across all entry points.

crossModuleConcerns.title: readRequestCache and overviewCache unbounded growth mentioned in BACKLOG deferred findings
crossModuleConcerns.description: The BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A documents that `readRequestCache` / `overviewCache` in the core-intelligence library layer have no eviction policy and grow without bound. This could manifest as a memory issue on long-running sessions where users navigate between many different intelligence scopes. The performance agent should verify whether the cache growth is observable in practice and whether the existing warm-cache/stale-while-revalidate pattern exacerbates it.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: XB-ARCHITECTURE (cache eviction design), XC-PERFORMANCE (memory growth under sustained use)

crossModuleConcerns.title: renderedTimeResults O(N log N) re-sort on every favicon/og mutation
crossModuleConcerns.description: The BACKLOG deferred findings note that `renderedTimeResults` combined with `groupEntriesByDay` re-sorts the entire accumulated scroll set on every favicon or og:image cache mutation. In the Explorer, favicon and og:image hydration happen continuously as the user scrolls, so this triggers repeated full-list rebuilds. This is a hot-path performance concern that the performance review agent should investigate with large page counts.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: XC-PERFORMANCE (Explorer scroll performance at scale), XB-ARCHITECTURE (decoupling icon hydration from grouped data)

crossModuleConcerns.title: Schedule page approaching 1,400-line hard limit blocks future work
crossModuleConcerns.description: The schedule route at 1,308 lines is 92 lines from the absolute hard limit where AGENTS.md prohibits adding any new business logic. Any future schedule feature (new platform support, interval customization improvements, schedule health enhancements) would be blocked until the maintainability review (WORK-SCHEDULE-PAGE-MAINT-A) is completed. The architecture agent should flag this as an immediate-term blocker for the schedule domain.

focusArea: UX Quality Review (XA-UX) -- All src/pages/, src/components/, src/styles/, src/app/ shell components
summary: Reviewed the full user-facing surface of PathKeep across shell chrome (sidebar, topbar, status bar, search palette), all 13 route pages, shared primitives (empty/error/loading/skeleton/busy-overlay/status-callout), the Explorer paper component family, intelligence components, heatmap components, review/progress/cards components, and the complete styles layer (tokens, paper, tailwind theme, and all app CSS modules). The paper-redesign aesthetic is well-executed and remarkably thorough: the three-font system, cream paper palette, editorial density, and CJK locale font stacks all align with the design-tokens contract. The i18n infrastructure appears complete with all user-facing copy going through the t() catalog. However, several UX quality and accessibility issues merit attention: contrast ratios for secondary ink tokens fall below WCAG AA in both themes; multiple legacy routes still use v0.2 CSS class patterns creating visual inconsistency; the status bar source picker lacks keyboard accessibility and ARIA semantics; and certain state transitions (particularly around schedule, audit, and assistant pages) use the non-paper page-shell class while the rest of the app has migrated to the paper outer wrapper convention.

findings.title: ink-faint and ink-ghost tokens fail WCAG AA contrast requirements in both themes
findings.description: Computed contrast ratios: light mode ink-faint (#beb3a2) on bg-paper (#f6f3ed) = 1.87:1, ink-ghost (#d5cdc0) on bg-paper = 1.42:1. Dark mode ink-faint (#4f4639) on bg-paper (#191614) = 1.94:1, ink-muted (#6a6055) on bg-paper = 2.93:1. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text. These tokens are used for metadata, timestamps, epigraphs, build versions, and mono kickers -- all small text that falls well below the minimum. The dark mode ink-muted at 2.93:1 also fails AA for the 10-11px mono text it is frequently used at (sidebar status, filter chips, section labels).
findings.impact: Users with low vision or in suboptimal lighting conditions cannot read timestamps, metadata, build versions, section labels, or epigraph text. This affects real usability since timestamps and domain names are critical for a history browser. Approximately 50+ component instances across the app use these tokens for readable text.
findings.suggestedFix: Adjust ink-faint in light mode from #beb3a2 to approximately #8a8070 (achieving ~3.5:1 against bg-paper) and in dark mode from #4f4639 to approximately #7a6d5e. Adjust ink-ghost to be reserved only for decorative, non-text elements (aria-hidden borders, dividers) -- audit all current usages and promote text usages to ink-faint or ink-muted. Consider adding a design-tokens.md note that ink-ghost must never carry readable text.
findings.codeSnippet:
--ink-faint: #beb3a2; /* 1.87:1 contrast on bg-paper */
--ink-ghost: #d5cdc0; /* 1.42:1 contrast on bg-paper */

findings.title: Assistant, Audit, and Security pages still use legacy page-shell / panel CSS classes, breaking paper aesthetic continuity
findings.description: While Dashboard, Explorer, Intelligence, Settings, Import, Jobs, Maintenance, Integrations, Onboarding, Lock, and Schedule all use the paper outer wrapper convention (mx-auto max-w-[1080px] flex flex-col pt-7 or PaperCard), the Assistant page at lines 323, 340, 360, and 419 still wraps all content in <section className='page-shell'> and uses .panel / .panel-header / .panel-body / .panel-action / .dashboard-next-action / .result-row / .intelligence-job-list CSS classes. The Audit page similarly uses page-shell (lines 376, 384, 395, 412, 434) with .panel class patterns. These are v0.2 CSS constructs that produce a visibly different visual rhythm -- no paper card borders, different padding, different background -- creating 'style islands' in the navigation flow.
findings.impact: Navigating from Dashboard or Explorer (paper aesthetic) into Assistant or Audit feels like entering a different application. The visual discontinuity undermines the 'book you want to open on Sunday afternoon' goal and erodes trust in polish. The deferred v0.3 badge on Assistant partially mitigates this for that route, but Audit is a shipping route.
findings.suggestedFix: For Audit: migrate from page-shell + panel classes to the paper outer wrapper + PaperCard composition that the other Operations routes use (Import, Jobs). For Assistant: since the AI features are v0.3-deferred and the current disabled state still displays, at minimum wrap the deferred-state callout in a PaperCard and use the paper outer wrapper container. The full active-AI conversation surface can wait for v0.3. Track in BACKLOG if not addressed this milestone.

findings.title: Status bar source picker popover lacks keyboard accessibility and ARIA semantics
findings.description: The source picker inside the status bar renders as a list of <button> elements inside a Radix Popover, but the container lacks role='listbox' or role='menu' semantics. There is no aria-label on the popover content area. The checkmark indicator (line 217, 254) uses a text character without aria-label or aria-checked semantics on the button. There is no keyboard arrow-key navigation between sources -- users must Tab through each option. The trigger button at line 151-175 has no aria-expanded attribute to communicate the popover state (though Radix may inject this, the trigger button itself is rendered via asChild on a bare <button>).
findings.impact: Screen reader users cannot efficiently navigate the source picker or understand the current selection state. The source picker is a globally-visible shell control that changes the scope of every page in the app -- its inaccessibility affects the core shared-profile-scope workflow.
findings.suggestedFix: Add role='listbox' to the source list container and role='option' with aria-selected={isSelected} to each source button. Add aria-label to the PopoverContent describing its purpose (e.g., t('shell.sourcesPickerLabel')). Ensure the checkmark character is wrapped in aria-hidden='true' since the selection state should be communicated via aria-selected. Consider adding arrow-key navigation via a useRovingTabIndex pattern.

findings.title: tokens.css color values drift from design-tokens.md contract in multiple places
findings.description: The design-tokens.md contract specifies light mode values: --bg-paper #ece7de, --bg-card-paper #f6f3ed, --bg-page #fdfcf9, --ink #171513, --ink-secondary #3c3a36, --ink-muted #6a655c, --ink-faint #9a948a, --accent #3d5a80, --danger #a03821. The implementation in tokens.css uses: --bg-page #ece7de (swapped name! doc says bg-paper is #ece7de), --bg-paper #f6f3ed (doc says bg-card-paper is #f6f3ed), --bg-card #fdfcf9 (doc says bg-page is #fdfcf9), --ink #1c1814, --ink-secondary #4a4139, --ink-muted #887d6e, --ink-faint #beb3a2, --accent-color #3d5a80 (matches), --error #a84040 (doc says --danger #a03821). The name mapping is inverted: what the doc calls 'bg-paper (main page background)' is what CSS calls '--bg-page', and what the doc calls 'bg-page (elevated surface)' is what CSS calls '--bg-card'. The actual hex values also differ for ink tokens.
findings.impact: While the visual output may be intentional (the implementation may be the living truth), the doc explicitly states: 'If tokens.css and this file disagree, fix tokens.css to match this contract -- not the other way around.' This contradiction means either the doc or the code needs updating. New contributors referencing the doc will use wrong values.
findings.suggestedFix: Audit whether the current tokens.css values are the intended shipping values. If so, update design-tokens.md to match. If the doc values are authoritative, fix tokens.css. The semantic name inversion (bg-page vs bg-paper) should be reconciled in both places -- the doc says bg-paper is the main background, but the CSS uses bg-page as main background.

findings.title: Skeleton components use legacy v0.2 CSS class names that may not match paper aesthetic
findings.description: All skeleton variants (Skeleton, DashboardSkeleton, TableSkeleton, SkeletonExplorer, SkeletonExplorerResults, SkeletonInsights) use legacy v0.2 CSS class names: .skeleton, .skeleton--stat-card, .skeleton--table-row, .skeleton--block, .skeleton-table, .page-shell, .skeleton-block, .panel, .panel-header, .panel-body, .intelligence-stack, .stats-row, .dashboard-grid, .dashboard-left, .dashboard-right, .record-list, .record-group, .record-group-header, .explorer-grid--skeleton. Meanwhile, the actual rendered pages use paper token Tailwind classes (PaperCard, mx-auto max-w-[1080px], font-serif, etc). This means the loading skeleton layout does not match the final rendered layout, violating the ux-principles.md rule: 'Skeleton block must match the final content layout, avoiding layout shift after loading.'
findings.impact: When a page transitions from loading to ready state, there is a visible layout jump as the skeleton's v0.2 grid structure is replaced by the paper layout. This is most noticeable on Dashboard and Intelligence where the skeleton uses .stats-row + .dashboard-grid while the actual page uses Tailwind grid classes.
findings.suggestedFix: Rebuild the skeleton components to mirror the paper layout structure. DashboardSkeleton should use the same mx-auto max-w-[1080px] wrapper + grid-cols-1 lg:grid-cols-2 gap pattern that DashboardPage uses. SkeletonInsights should match the Intelligence page's paper card structure. This work could be batched with the next paper route sweep.

findings.title: BusyOverlay and LoadingState log lines use the logLine string as React key, risking duplicate-key warnings and incorrect reconciliation
findings.description: Both BusyOverlay (line 136) and LoadingState (line 99) render log lines with `key={line}`. Log lines from backend import/backup operations can easily contain identical strings (e.g., repeated progress messages like 'Processing Chrome profile...'), which would produce duplicate React keys. This causes React to log console warnings and potentially skip rendering some lines or misorder them.
findings.impact: During import or backup operations -- the most trust-critical moments in PathKeep -- users might see incorrect or missing progress information, undermining the PME transparency principle.
findings.suggestedFix: Use the array index combined with the line content for the key, e.g., `key={`log-${index}-${line.slice(0,20)}`}`, or better, switch to a stable incrementing counter that is part of the log event data model from the backend.
findings.codeSnippet:
{logLines.slice(-4).map((line) => (
  <div key={line} className="busy-overlay__step busy-overlay__step--active">

findings.title: EmptyState and ErrorState primitives use v0.2 CSS classes (.utility-block, .mono-kicker) without paper token integration
findings.description: EmptyState renders with className='utility-block' and ErrorState with className='utility-block utility-block--danger'. Both use .mono-kicker for the eyebrow. These are v0.2 CSS class patterns that do not use the paper token system (no font-serif, no bg-card-paper, no rounded-paper, no border-border-light). These primitives appear on every route's empty/error/locked/uninitialized state paths, making them one of the most frequently encountered surfaces in the app -- yet they are the least paper-consistent.
findings.impact: The first thing a new user sees (empty archive, locked state fallback) looks visually different from the rest of the paper redesign. This breaks the 'a book you want to open on a Sunday afternoon' impression at the most critical first-contact moment.
findings.suggestedFix: Rewrite EmptyState and ErrorState to use Tailwind paper utilities: font-serif for the title, font-mono for the eyebrow, bg-card-paper or bg-paper for the container, border-border-light for any borders, and the paper radius. The StatusCallout primitive is already closer to this pattern and could serve as a structural reference.

findings.title: Year heatmap cells lack keyboard accessibility -- no focusable individual cells or arrow-key navigation
findings.description: The year heatmap renders ~365 cells, but examining the component structure (LEVEL_CLASS map, onSelectDate handler), individual cells appear to be rendered without tabIndex or keyboard event handlers. The cells use title attributes for tooltips but clicking a day to drill into Explorer requires a mouse click. There is no aria-label on cells describing the date and count for screen readers, and no keyboard arrow-key grid navigation pattern (WAI-ARIA grid or application role).
findings.impact: Keyboard-only users cannot interact with the heatmap -- a prominent Dashboard feature. The heatmap is a key 'browsing rhythm' surface referenced in the design docs as a trust-critical visual. The ux-principles.md mandates that 'high-risk flows must be keyboard-only walkable'. While heatmap is not high-risk, the Dashboard is the landing page.
findings.suggestedFix: Add tabIndex={0} to cells with non-zero counts, with onKeyDown handling Enter/Space to trigger onSelectDate. Add aria-label to each cell using the copy.cellTooltip function output. Consider implementing WAI-ARIA grid pattern for efficient arrow-key navigation, or at minimum add role='img' with an aria-label on the entire heatmap summarizing the date range and total.

findings.title: PaperSearchEmpty recent-meta uses string template replacement instead of i18n interpolation
findings.description: The recent search metadata line uses manual .replace('{mode}', entry.mode).replace('{count}', String(entry.count)).replace('{when}', entry.when) instead of the t() interpolation system that the rest of the app uses. This bypasses the i18n locale formatting (e.g., number formatting for count, mode name localization) and means the mode string will always display the English internal identifier ('keyword', 'regex', 'semantic') rather than a localized label.
findings.impact: zh-CN and zh-TW users will see English mode identifiers mixed into Chinese text in the recent searches surface. This is a minor i18n contract violation per AGENTS.md rule 5.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace the manual string template with a t() call that handles interpolation and localizes the mode name. For example: t('explorer.recentMeta', { mode: t(`explorer.mode_${entry.mode}`), count: entry.count, when: entry.when }).
findings.codeSnippet:
{copy.recentMeta
  .replace('{mode}', entry.mode)
  .replace('{count}', String(entry.count))
  .replace('{when}', entry.when)}

findings.title: PKTopbar subtitle is hidden on narrow viewports with sm:inline but no alternative is shown
findings.description: The page subtitle in the topbar uses className='hidden truncate font-sans text-[12px] text-ink-faint sm:inline', meaning on viewports narrower than the sm breakpoint (640px), the subtitle is completely hidden. While the sidebar has its collapsed state for narrow windows, the topbar does not provide any compensating context -- users see just the page title with no additional orientation. The docs/design spec says 'desktop-first, but preserve narrow window usability.'
findings.impact: Users who resize the PathKeep window below 640px lose the route subtitle context. This is a real scenario for side-by-side desktop workflows. The impact is minor since the serif title alone is still informative.
findings.suggestedFix: Consider showing the subtitle as a tooltip on the title element when the window is narrow, or as a mono tag next to the title. Alternatively, accept this as intentional density reduction for narrow viewports and document it.

findings.title: StatusCallout uses v0.2 CSS class pattern (.status-callout--{tone}) instead of paper token Tailwind utilities
findings.description: StatusCallout renders with className={`status-callout status-callout--${tone}`} and uses .mono-kicker / .utility-block__actions. While StatusCallout is used across the entire app (Lock, Onboarding, Schedule, Import, Security, Settings, Explorer), it still relies on v0.2 CSS class definitions rather than Tailwind paper token utilities. The rendered output depends on whatever .status-callout is defined as in the legacy CSS layer, which may not use the correct paper border-radius (3px), paper card background, or serif/sans/mono font assignments.
findings.impact: Every warning, info, and danger callout across the app may have subtly inconsistent styling compared to the surrounding PaperCard-based content. Since StatusCallout appears in trust-critical contexts (lock screen recovery, schedule warnings, import errors), visual consistency here directly affects perceived reliability.
findings.suggestedFix: Rewrite StatusCallout to use Tailwind paper token classes directly: bg-success-soft/bg-warning-soft/bg-error-soft/bg-info-soft for the background tint, border-success/warning/error/info for the left or full border, rounded-paper for corners, font-serif for the title, font-sans for the body. This aligns with the design-tokens.md 'Semantic Tone Tokens' section.

findings.title: Schedule page at 1308 lines exceeds the 1200-line review threshold and is the only remaining oversized route file
findings.description: The schedule page is 1308 lines, exceeding the AGENTS.md 1200-line review threshold. BACKLOG already tracks this as WORK-SCHEDULE-PAGE-MAINT-A. From a UX perspective, the file contains inline state-panel rendering for all schedule states (CHECKING, NOT_INSTALLED, INSTALLED_OK, INSTALLED_WARN, INSTALLED_ERROR) plus manual step rendering, interval selection, profile summary, and diagnostics -- all in one component. This makes it harder to ensure consistent UX treatment across states and harder to verify that all states have proper loading/error/empty handling.
findings.impact: The monolithic structure makes it likely that UX improvements to one schedule state (e.g., adding paper card wrappers) will miss another state. The BACKLOG already deferred paper inner-panel styling for schedule specifically because the file is too large to safely modify.
findings.suggestedFix: This is already tracked in BACKLOG as WORK-SCHEDULE-PAGE-MAINT-A. No additional action needed beyond noting that the UX review confirms the maintainability concern has direct UX implications.

commendations.title: Exemplary three-font system implementation with full CJK locale support
commendations.description: The typography token layer in tokens.css is thorough and thoughtful. The three-font system (Newsreader serif for editorial headings, system sans for UI chrome, JetBrains Mono for evidence/paths) is consistently applied across the shell and paper components. The CJK locale overrides (:root:lang(zh-CN), :root:lang(zh-TW)) correctly replace both serif and sans stacks with appropriate platform-native Chinese typefaces (PingFang, Microsoft YaHei/JhengHei, Noto CJK). The data-fonts='system' fallback correctly strips bundled fonts while preserving the CJK locale stacks. This level of i18n typography care is rare and directly supports the product's commitment to en/zh-CN/zh-TW as shipping languages.

commendations.title: Paper materiality layer achieves the editorial aesthetic without performance cost
commendations.description: The paper.css materiality layer is elegant: SVG fractalNoise at 2.8% opacity for paper texture, a CSS radial-gradient vignette for darkroom mode (gated by --vignette-opacity), and a comprehensive reduced-motion media query that collapses all animations to 0.001ms while preserving the materiality opacity tokens. The noise texture uses an inline SVG data URI to avoid a network request, and it is applied via a fixed pseudo-element on #root with pointer-events:none so it never interferes with interaction. The ::selection styling honors the accent token. The :focus-visible ring uses accent color with 2px offset. All keyframes (skeleton shimmer, detail slide-in, background progress glide) are restrained and editorial -- no bouncy physics or spring animations. This is exactly what the design-tokens.md 'motion stays restrained' contract asks for.

commendations.title: Explorer Browse contact sheet is a standout paper aesthetic implementation
commendations.description: The PaperContactFrame, PaperListRow, PaperDayHeader, and PaperContactSheet components demonstrate exceptional attention to the paper editorial aesthetic. The contact frame's FallbackPanel -- which converts the empty hero area into a serif 'title page' with a domain word-mark and mono ledger row when neither favicon nor og:image exists -- is a creative solution to the common 'fresh takeout import' scenario where half the archive has no icons. The day header correctly uses CSS variable --pk-toolbar-h for sticky positioning (following the recent fix that eliminated the ResizeObserver-to-setState delay), and the filmstrip index numbering (padStart(2, '0')) adds a considered editorial detail. The three-font assignment is consistent: serif for titles, sans for UI text, mono for domains and timestamps. The 1.91:1 aspect ratio on card images matches the Open Graph standard for social cards.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Legacy v0.2 CSS class consumption is widespread and should be inventoried
crossModuleConcerns.description: Multiple routes (Assistant, Audit, Security error states) and all shared primitives (EmptyState, ErrorState, LoadingState, StatusCallout, BusyOverlay, Skeleton variants) still consume v0.2 CSS class names (.page-shell, .panel, .panel-header, .panel-body, .utility-block, .status-callout, .mono-kicker, .skeleton, .dashboard-grid, .intelligence-stack, .result-row, etc). These are defined in src/styles/app/*.css and render with the legacy v0.2 visual language. A cross-cutting inventory of which components still consume legacy classes vs. paper Tailwind utilities would help prioritize the remaining paper migration work and ensure no 'style islands' persist into release.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: All src/components/primitives/, src/pages/assistant/, src/pages/audit/, src/pages/security/ error paths, src/styles/app/ CSS modules

crossModuleConcerns.title: Color token naming inversion between design-tokens.md and tokens.css needs reconciliation
crossModuleConcerns.description: The design-tokens.md contract uses bg-paper for the main page background (#ece7de) and bg-page for the elevated surface (#fdfcf9), but tokens.css inverts this: --bg-page is #ece7de (main background) and --bg-paper is #f6f3ed (card-level surface). The Tailwind theme (tailwind.css) maps --color-page to var(--bg-page) and --color-paper to var(--bg-paper), propagating the inversion. The actual ink hex values also differ (doc: #171513, CSS: #1c1814). The doc states CSS should match the doc, not vice versa. This discrepancy should be resolved by the design/architecture review to determine which is the living truth.
crossModuleConcerns.relatedAreas: docs/design/design-tokens.md, src/styles/tokens.css, src/styles/tailwind.css, all components using bg-page / bg-paper Tailwind utilities

crossModuleConcerns.title: Background task progress rendering should be reviewed for 14.4M record import scenarios
crossModuleConcerns.description: The BusyOverlay and BackgroundProgress components handle import/backup progress, but log lines use the line string content as React keys (risking duplicates during long imports) and the log window is hard-coded to show the last 4 lines. For a 14.4M record import that could run for hours, the progress surface should be evaluated for: (a) whether the 4-line window provides enough diagnostic context, (b) whether the progress percentage updates are granular enough to distinguish stalled vs. progressing, (c) whether the structured logEvents from the backend are being consumed (the ux-principles.md requires 'production UI should prefer structured progress logEvents' over raw backend log lines).

summary: Systematic review of all IPC/Tauri invoke paths, SQL queries, list/table/scroll rendering, Rust import/intelligence/migration pipelines, and frontend caching for 14.4M-record performance at the 4-core/8GB target. Found 12 findings across all five performance dimensions. The regex recall path is the most critical: it materializes the entire visits table before filtering, which will OOM or freeze on any non-trivial archive. The intelligence structural aggregate builders carry unbounded visit_ids Vec per refind-page URL with no cap, and the frontend readRequestCache / overviewCache grow without eviction. On the positive side, the cursor-based pagination contract, the IntersectionObserver-driven viewport recycling, and the 64 KiB streaming migration pipeline are all well-designed for the 14.4M target.

findings.title: Regex recall path materializes entire visits table into memory
findings.description: list_history_with_regex passes :pageLimit = -1 (unbounded) to LIST_HISTORY_SQL, collecting every visible visit row into a Vec, then post-filters with the regex in Rust. On a 14.4M-row archive this allocates ~4.3 GB of HistoryEntry objects before a single row is shown to the user. Even on the 264k-row dev archive this is already noticeable; at the target scale it will OOM or hang for many seconds.
findings.codeSnippet:
":pageLimit": -1i64,
// ...
let filtered_items = rows
    .collect::<rusqlite::Result<Vec<_>>>()?
    .into_iter()
    .filter(|entry| { regex.is_match(&entry.url) ... })
    .collect::<Vec<_>>();
findings.impact: Complete UI freeze or OOM crash when a user uses regex search on any archive larger than ~500k visits. The 14.4M target makes this path unusable.
findings.suggestedFix: Switch to a bounded candidate scan: stream rows in batches of 1000-5000, apply the regex filter per batch, stop once the page window is filled plus one lookahead. Add a response field like scannedRows so the frontend can show an honest 'scanned N rows, showing M matches -- narrow your filter' message in all three languages. This is already tracked in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A as 'History regex recall unbounded'.

findings.title: Unbounded visit_ids Vec in refind-page structural aggregate accumulator
findings.description: RefindAccumulatorEntry carries a visit_ids: Vec<i64> that grows without limit. For a popular page visited daily over 20 years the vector can hold hundreds of thousands of visit IDs. These are serialized into the evidence_json string via serde_json::json!({"visitIds": entry.visit_ids}). On a 14.4M archive with popular pages, this produces multi-megabyte JSON blobs per refind-page row and keeps millions of i64 values in memory across all accumulated refind pages simultaneously.
findings.impact: Hundreds of MB of memory consumed during structural rebuild on a large archive; persisted JSON blobs waste disk and slow row reads. Already noted in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A.
findings.suggestedFix: Cap visit_ids at a reasonable sample size (e.g., 50 most recent) and record totalVisitCount as a separate integer field. The evidence JSON only needs enough IDs for UI drill-down, not the full set.

findings.title: readRequestCache and overviewCache grow without eviction
findings.description: readRequestCache and overviewCache are module-scoped Map instances that are never evicted except on explicit clearIntelligenceCache() calls. Every unique (command, args) combination adds an entry. As the user navigates different date ranges, profiles, and section views across a long session, these maps grow unboundedly. The overview cache stores full CoreIntelligencePrimaryOverview / SecondaryOverview payloads which can be sizable on large archives.
findings.impact: On an 8 GB machine running for hours, the JS heap grows monotonically. No per-entry TTL or LRU eviction means stale data can also silently hide post-rebuild changes if clearIntelligenceCache is not called.
findings.suggestedFix: Add LRU eviction with a bounded capacity (e.g., 20-50 entries for readRequestCache, 5-10 for overviewCache). A simple approach: check size on set and evict the oldest entries. This is already noted in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A.

findings.title: O(N) re-aggregation of entire accumulated entry set on every page fetch
findings.description: Every time a new page is loaded by the infinite scroll hook, combinedTimeResults spreads all items into a new array [...visibleTimeResults.items, ...infiniteExtraItems], then renderedTimeResults maps over the entire combined array to hydrate favicon/og-image lookups. At MAX_ACCUMULATED_PAGES=500 with ~50 entries per page, this is ~25,000 entries being copied and mapped on every page fetch. The extraItems memo inside use-explorer-infinite-pages.ts (line 350-357) also iterates all buffered pages on every pageItems state change.
findings.impact: At 500 accumulated pages (~25k entries), each page fetch triggers ~75,000 array operations (spread + map + extraItems rebuild). On the 4-core target this causes per-fetch jank that grows linearly with scroll depth. Already documented in the browse-virt-spike-2026-05-25.md as the reason the cap was lowered from 1000 to 500.
findings.suggestedFix: Switch to an incremental aggregator: append new page entries to combinedTimeResults instead of rebuilding from scratch. The favicon/og hydration map lookup can be done lazily per visible row (matching the viewport recycling pattern already in place) rather than eagerly for 25k rows. This is tracked as a prerequisite for raising MAX_ACCUMULATED_PAGES.

findings.title: Offset-based pagination used when numbered pages are requested
findings.description: When the frontend requests a numbered page (requested_page.is_some()), the query falls back to OFFSET :pageOffset instead of cursor-based keyset pagination. For page N with 50 rows per page, SQLite must scan and discard (N-1)*50 rows before returning results. At page 500 of a 14.4M archive, that means scanning and discarding ~25,000 rows even with an index. The cursor path is only used when requested_page is None. The lexical search path has the same pattern.
findings.codeSnippet: let page_offset = if requested_page.is_some() { i64::try_from(start_index).unwrap_or(i64::MAX) } else { 0 };
findings.impact: Deep-page jumps (e.g., page 1000+ in a 14.4M archive) degrade to O(offset) scan time. The infinite-scroll browse path uses cursor mode and is unaffected, but the explicit page-number pagination surface (added in WORK-V03-CODEX-REVIEW-FOLLOWUP) does hit this path.
findings.suggestedFix: When a numbered page is requested, translate the page number into a cursor by computing the visit_time_ms threshold from the offset via a single indexed COUNT query, then use keyset pagination from that threshold. Alternatively, limit the maximum requestable page number to a reasonable bound (e.g., 200) and rely on cursor-based infinite scroll for deeper access.

findings.title: load_visible_visits materializes all profile visits for scoped-debug rebuild
findings.description: load_visible_visits loads ALL visible visits for one profile into a Vec<VisitRecord> before the limit parameter trims to the tail. On a 14.4M archive with a single profile, this collects the entire table into memory. Even with the limit parameter, it first collects all rows then calls split_off to keep only the last `limit` entries (line 68-76 trim_visits_to_limit). The production rebuild path calls this at intelligence_rebuild.rs:284 for 'scoped debug fallback' rebuilds.
findings.impact: A scoped-debug rebuild on a single-profile 14.4M archive loads all 14.4M VisitRecord structs into memory before trimming. Each VisitRecord is ~300+ bytes, totaling ~4+ GB. This is already tracked in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A.
findings.suggestedFix: Push the LIMIT into the SQL ORDER BY ... DESC LIMIT ?2 and reverse in Rust, or use the existing batched load_profile_derived_visit_batch path. The scoped-debug path should use the same bounded loading as the production incremental path.

findings.title: PRAGMA quick_check runs full-database scan on every Browser Direct import/preview
findings.description: quick_check_sqlite runs PRAGMA quick_check (an unbounded full-page integrity scan) on the staged browser History database during every import and preview operation. For a Chrome History database with years of data this can take several seconds. The check runs synchronously before the import begins.
findings.impact: Adds 1-5 seconds of blocking I/O to every Browser Direct import preview and execution on a large browser History database. Already tracked in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A.
findings.suggestedFix: Replace with PRAGMA quick_check(1) which stops after the first error and returns in constant time, or use PRAGMA integrity_check(1) for a faster variant. Alternatively, skip the check on preview and only run it on execute, since the staging copy validates the snapshot anyway.

findings.title: AI index candidate collection loads all visits when limit is None
findings.description: collect_visits_to_index has a code path where limit is None, in which case the SQL has no LIMIT clause and collects all visible visits into Vec<IndexedVisit>. While the v0.2 code bails with an error before using these candidates (line 170-172), the candidates are already fully materialized in memory before the bail. The bail happens after collect_visits_to_index returns.
findings.impact: In v0.2 the bail prevents further processing, but the full visits table is still loaded into memory before the bail. If v0.3 removes the bail, this becomes a critical unbounded allocation. On a 14.4M archive: ~14.4M IndexedVisit objects at ~200 bytes each = ~2.9 GB.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the v0.3 bail check before collect_visits_to_index so no memory is allocated. When v0.3 re-enables AI indexing, ensure collect_visits_to_index always applies a batch limit and streams batches.

findings.title: Intelligence job recovery has no attempt cap -- expired jobs requeue indefinitely
findings.description: recover_expired_intelligence_jobs requeues all running jobs whose lease has expired, resetting them to 'queued' state unconditionally. There is no attempt counter check, so a job that consistently fails within the lease period (e.g., crashes after 200s on a 300s lease) will be requeued indefinitely. Each retry consumes CPU on the target 4-core machine for potentially expensive rebuild computations.
findings.codeSnippet:
SET state = 'queued', ... last_error = 'PathKeep recovered an expired intelligence lease.'
WHERE state = 'running' AND lease_expires_at <= ?2
findings.impact: A consistently-failing intelligence job creates an infinite retry loop, burning CPU on each attempt. On the 4-core target machine this can starve user-facing queries. Already tracked in BACKLOG WORK-REVIEW-0612-FOLLOWUPS-A.
findings.suggestedFix: Add an attempt column check: only requeue if attempt < MAX_ATTEMPTS (e.g., 3-5). Move permanently-failing jobs to 'failed' state with a clear error message.

findings.title: Intelligence runtime snapshot includes full dirty_date_keys per job over IPC
findings.description: Every IntelligenceJobOverview in the runtime snapshot carries dirty_date_keys: Vec<String>, which can contain up to ~400 date strings per job (observed on the 264k dev archive). The shell sidebar only needs queue counts and the running job title, but load_intelligence_runtime returns the full snapshot with all recent jobs and their dirty_date_keys over IPC. This is already documented in BACKLOG as WORK-PERF-RUNTIME-LITE-A with a measured 2.9s cold load.
findings.codeSnippet: dirty_date_keys: artifact.dirty_date_keys,
findings.impact: Shell mount takes ~2.9s on a 264k archive due to oversized IPC payload. At 14.4M this will be worse. The sidebar never reads dirty_date_keys.
findings.suggestedFix: Split into load_intelligence_runtime_digest (lightweight: queue counts, running job title/progress) for the shell, and the full snapshot for the /intelligence route. This is exactly the design proposed in WORK-PERF-RUNTIME-LITE-A.

findings.title: COUNT_HISTORY_SQL and COUNT_HISTORY_LEXICAL_SQL perform full-table scans on every page fetch
findings.description: Both the SQL and lexical query paths issue a separate COUNT(*) query (COUNT_HISTORY_SQL / COUNT_HISTORY_LEXICAL_SQL) before fetching the actual page. These COUNT queries must scan the entire matched result set to return total. On a 14.4M archive with broad date ranges and no text filter, this scans all visible visits. The count is needed for the page_count / has_previous pagination metadata, but it doubles the query cost per page fetch.
findings.codeSnippet: let total: usize = connection.query_row(COUNT_HISTORY_SQL, ..., |row| row.get::<_, i64>(0))?;
findings.impact: Each page navigation triggers two full query plans: one for count, one for rows. On a 14.4M archive without date filters, the COUNT pass alone could take 1-3 seconds on encrypted SQLite.
findings.suggestedFix: For cursor-based infinite scroll (the primary Browse path), the count is not needed -- has_next can be determined by fetching limit+1 rows. Only compute total when the UI explicitly needs page count (numbered pagination mode). Consider caching the count across pages within the same query signature.

findings.title: useBrowseDayInsightsCache fires IPC side effects from resolve() during render
findings.description: The resolve() function returned by useBrowseDayInsightsCache is called from inside the contact sheet render path (via PaperDayHeader). When a day has not been cached yet, resolve() mutates the cache Map (line 122: cacheState.cache.set) and fires a backend.getBrowseDayInsights IPC call as a side effect during render. While this works because the mutation is on a mutable Map reference (not React state), it violates the React principle of pure renders and can cause double-fetches in StrictMode dev builds.
findings.codeSnippet:
cacheState.cache.set(date, { state: 'pending' })
backend.getBrowseDayInsights({ date, profileId: ... })
findings.impact: In development StrictMode, double render causes double IPC calls for each day. In production the dedup via the pending state mostly prevents this, but the pattern is fragile under concurrent render.
findings.suggestedFix: Move the fetch trigger into a useEffect that observes a 'requested dates' queue, or use a ref-based pending tracker that does not depend on render-time mutation. The resolve() function should remain pure, returning cached data or null, while a separate effect drives the fetch.

commendations.title: Cursor-based pagination contract is well-designed for scale
commendations.description: The HistoryCursor enum with Chronological and Relevance variants, combined with the keyset pagination clauses in LIST_HISTORY_SQL and LIST_HISTORY_LEXICAL_SQL, provides O(1) page access regardless of depth. The cursor encodes (visit_time_ms, id) or (score, visit_time_ms, id) which maps directly to indexed columns. This is exactly the right pagination strategy for the 14.4M target and is the primary path used by the infinite scroll surface.

commendations.title: IntersectionObserver viewport-driven day recycling keeps DOM bounded
commendations.description: The paper-contact-sheet implements viewport-driven recycling via IntersectionObserver on each day block. Off-screen day blocks collapse to a height-preserving placeholder div (data-virt-state='recycled'), keeping mounted DOM nodes proportional to viewport size rather than total accumulated pages. Combined with the BROWSE-VIRT spike measurements documenting ~6.25 nodes/row (list) and ~14.25 nodes/row (cards), the target of ~400-580 mounted DOM nodes is well-grounded in empirical data. This is the correct architecture for a 14.4M-row browser.

commendations.title: Migration pipeline streams through 64 KiB buffer for bounded memory
commendations.description: The export/import bundle pipeline (migration.rs) uses a 64 KiB streaming buffer (ZIP_COPY_BUFFER_BYTES) for both packing and unpacking, with per-file SHA-256 validation streamed through a Sha256 hasher. This means peak memory during bundle export/import stays bounded regardless of archive size. The explicit doc comment 'Both pack and unpack stream files through a 64 KiB buffer so peak memory stays bounded regardless of archive size' shows deliberate performance-aware design for the 14.4M-row target.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Intelligence rebuild path touches both vault-core SQL and vault-worker job orchestration
crossModuleConcerns.description: The unbounded load_visible_visits and visit_ids accumulation findings span intelligence_visit_records.rs, intelligence_structural_aggregates.rs, intelligence_rebuild.rs, and the worker job_runtime.rs. The fix requires coordinating the bounded batch loading strategy across all these modules simultaneously to avoid leaving half the path unbounded.

crossModuleConcerns.title: Frontend cache eviction policy needs alignment across multiple independent caches
crossModuleConcerns.description: readRequestCache, overviewCache (shared.ts), the useExplorerFavicons cache, the useExplorerOgImages cache, and the useBrowseDayInsightsCache all grow independently with no shared eviction coordination. A UX or security review should verify that clearIntelligenceCache() (which only clears the intelligence caches) is called at appropriate boundaries (archive unlock, profile switch, import), and that the explorer caches also have eviction paths.
